1	/**
2	 * Process Manager — interface and shared types.
3	 *
4	 * ProcessManager is the single abstraction the Pipeline Engine uses to start
5	 * a child process. Two implementations exist:
6	 *   JigProcessManager   — production: spawns whatever command + args the
7	 *                         caller supplies (always from ResolvedConfig).
8	 *   ScriptedProcessManager — replays recorded stream-json JSONL fixtures
9	 *                            (testability seam, see §Testability in plan-draft3).
10	 *
11	 * Callers program to ProcessManager + SpawnHandle. They never import from
12	 * jig-manager.ts directly so ScriptedProcessManager is a drop-in replacement.
13	 */
14	
15	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
16	// Typed error class
17	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
18	
19	/** Discriminated failure kinds raised by the process lifecycle. */
20	export type ProcessErrorKind = 'epipe' | 'spawn_failed' | 'stdin_error';
21	
22	/**
23	 * A named process-lifecycle failure, distinct from generic Node.js errors.
24	 *
25	 * Every catch block in process management code either handles a specific
26	 * ProcessErrorKind or re-raises as ProcessError — no swallowed errors.
27	 */
28	export class ProcessError extends Error {
29	  constructor(
30	    public readonly kind: ProcessErrorKind,
31	    public readonly cause: unknown,
32	    message?: string,
33	  ) {
34	    super(message ?? kind);
35	    this.name = 'ProcessError';
36	  }
37	}
38	
39	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
40	// SpawnOpts
41	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
42	
43	/**
44	 * Options passed to ProcessManager.spawn(). Command and args MUST come from
45	 * ResolvedConfig — never hard-coded strings.
46	 */
47	export interface SpawnOpts {
48	  /** Executable path or name — sourced from ResolvedConfig.phases[p].command. */
49	  command: string;
50	  /** Argument vector — sourced from ResolvedConfig.phases[p].args. */
51	  args: string[];
52	  /** Absolute working directory for the child process. */
53	  cwd: string;
54	  /** Additional environment variables merged over process.env. */
55	  env?: Record<string, string>;
56	  /** Prompt content written once to child stdin then the pipe is closed. */
57	  promptBuffer: string | Buffer;
58	  /**
59	   * Milliseconds to wait for the process to exit after SIGTERM before
60	   * escalating to SIGKILL. Production default: 10 000 ms.
61	   * Exposed so tests can use a much shorter value.
62	   */
63	  gracePeriodMs?: number;
64	}
65	
66	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
67	// SpawnHandle
68	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
69	
70	/**
71	 * Handle to a running child process returned by ProcessManager.spawn().
72	 *
73	 * Typed event signatures are declared via overloaded `on`/`once` so callers
74	 * get type-checked listener parameters. The concrete implementations are
75	 * EventEmitter subclasses; the interface does not import EventEmitter so
76	 * ScriptedProcessManager may use any backing mechanism.
77	 */
78	export interface SpawnHandle {
79	  /** OS PID of the child process. */
80	  readonly pid: number;
81	
82	  /**
83	   * Process group ID. With detached:true the child is its own pgid leader,
84	   * so pgid === pid on POSIX.
85	   * `process.kill(-pgid, signal)` sends the signal to the entire group.
86	   */
87	  readonly pgid: number;
88	
89	  /**
90	   * Liveness probe: `process.kill(pid, 0)`.
91	   * Returns true if the OS reports the process is alive, false if it has
92	   * exited or was never started successfully.
93	   */
94	  isAlive(): boolean;
95	
96	  /**
97	   * Cancel the running child with SIGTERM → SIGKILL escalation:
98	   *   1. `process.kill(-pgid, 'SIGTERM')` — signal entire process group.
99	   *   2. Wait up to `gracePeriodMs`.
100	   *   3. If still alive, `process.kill(-pgid, 'SIGKILL')`.
101	   * Resolves after SIGKILL is sent (or immediately if the process is already
102	   * dead). Does NOT wait for the 'exit' event to fire.
103	   */
104	  cancel(): Promise<void>;
105	
106	  // Typed event listeners.
107	  /** Raw stdout line delivered by the line-buffered reader (one NDJSON object). */
108	  on(event: 'stdout_line', listener: (line: string) => void): this;
109	  /** Chunk of stderr text (capped; see 'stderr_cap_reached'). */
110	  on(event: 'stderr_data', listener: (chunk: string) => void): this;
111	  /** Emitted once when accumulated stderr reaches the 64 KB cap. */
112	  on(event: 'stderr_cap_reached', listener: () => void): this;
113	  /** Process exited — code is null if killed by a signal. */
114	  on(
115	    event: 'exit',
116	    listener: (code: number | null, signal: NodeJS.Signals | null) => void,
117	  ): this;
118	  /** Typed process-lifecycle error (EPIPE, spawn_failed, stdin_error). */
119	  on(event: 'error', listener: (err: ProcessError) => void): this;
120	
121	  once(event: 'stdout_line', listener: (line: string) => void): this;
122	  once(event: 'stderr_data', listener: (chunk: string) => void): this;
123	  once(event: 'stderr_cap_reached', listener: () => void): this;
124	  once(
125	    event: 'exit',
126	    listener: (code: number | null, signal: NodeJS.Signals | null) => void,
127	  ): this;
128	  once(event: 'error', listener: (err: ProcessError) => void): this;
129	
130	  /** Remove a previously registered listener. */
131	  off(event: string, listener: (...args: any[]) => void): this;
132	}
133	
134	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
135	// ProcessManager interface
136	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
137	
138	/**
139	 * The single abstraction the Pipeline Engine uses to start a child process.
140	 * Both JigProcessManager (production) and ScriptedProcessManager (test replay)
141	 * implement this interface; no JigProcessManager-specific methods are exposed.
142	 */
143	export interface ProcessManager {
144	  spawn(opts: SpawnOpts): Promise<SpawnHandle>;
145	}
146

1	# Backend Critique — plan-draft2.md
2	
3	## Accept (already correct)
4	- Files-as-contract between phases (Core Design Principles #1) — dodges the hardest coordination problems and gives us durable artifacts to drive the state machine from. This is the right call for a harness that can crash and resume.
5	- Hooks own quality gates, not the harness (Core Design Principles #2, Hooks Integration) — right split. The harness would otherwise have to maintain a parallel truth about "did tests pass" and it would drift.
6	- SQLite via better-sqlite3 with synchronous API (Tech Stack) — correct for a single-writer orchestrator. Sync API removes an entire class of race conditions in the state machine and is a genuine fit here.
7	- Per-workflow worktrees (Worktree Management) — necessary for parallel workflows, and also the only clean way to do `-c` resume on a specific session tree.
8	- "No arbitrary timeouts" (Process Management § No Arbitrary Timeouts) — correct instinct. A wall-clock timeout on a long build is how you corrupt a worktree mid-write. Heartbeat on stdout activity is the right signal.
9	- Capturing raw stream-json to disk (`retain_stream_json: true` in logging config) — the right default. When something breaks mid-run, the raw transcript is the only source of truth.
10	- Planner defines `review_criteria` alongside `acceptance_criteria` (File Contract § features.json) — anti-rubber-stamp mechanism that doesn't require harness-side cleverness.
11	- Optional pre-step usage gate is opt-in (Usage Tracking § Optional Pre-Step Usage Check) — keeping this out of the hot path is correct; rate-limit management is a swamp.
12	
13	## Challenge (propose revision)
14	
15	### stream-json parsing assumes line-delimited JSON without saying so
16	**Issue:** Process Management § Streaming shows `child.stdout.on('data', ...)` with a comment "Parse stream-json chunks." Node's `'data'` event fires on arbitrary byte boundaries — a single JSON event can be split across two `data` chunks, and two events can arrive in one chunk. The plan never specifies the framing (NDJSON? length-prefixed? concatenated objects?) or the buffering strategy. "Parse each chunk" as written will break on the first large tool_use event that straddles a read boundary.
17	**Impact:** Silent data loss in the UI stream, corrupted token-usage accounting, and crash-on-malformed-JSON the first time an event exceeds the pipe buffer (~64KB on macOS). This will not show up in dev and will show up in production the moment an agent pastes a large file into a tool call.
18	**Proposed change:** Commit to Claude Code's stream-json being NDJSON (needs verification — see Questions), and specify a line-buffered reader: accumulate bytes in a `Buffer`, split on `\n`, parse complete lines, carry the trailing partial line forward. Use `readline.createInterface({ input: child.stdout, crlfDelay: Infinity })` or a hand-rolled splitter — do NOT JSON.parse raw `data` chunks. Also specify behavior for: (a) a line that fails JSON.parse (log + skip, don't crash the pipeline), (b) a line exceeding a sanity cap (e.g., 16MB — truncate and mark the session tainted), (c) stderr interleaving (stderr is a separate stream, never mixed into the JSON parser).
19	
20	### "Pipe assembled prompt via stdin" ignores EPIPE and backpressure
21	**Issue:** Process Management § Spawning shows `promptStream.pipe(child.stdin)` with no error handling. If jig/claude exits before consuming the whole prompt (fast error path, auth failure, profile-not-found, rate limit at startup), the write side gets EPIPE and crashes the harness unless explicitly handled. Additionally, large prompts (multi-MB features.json + architecture.md + progress.md + recent diff) can hit the OS pipe buffer and block writes; if the child hasn't started draining yet the harness deadlocks waiting for `drain`.
22	**Impact:** Harness crashes or hangs on the first unlucky phase start. Hard to reproduce, hard to debug.
23	**Proposed change:** (1) Attach `child.stdin.on('error', ...)` before piping and treat EPIPE as "child died during prompt send — fail the phase, capture stderr, don't crash." (2) Don't stream the prompt from a file — read it into memory (we already have the whole thing) and `child.stdin.end(promptBuffer)` in one call. (3) Document the prompt size ceiling and validate at assembly time (reject prompts > N MB with a clear error rather than deadlocking). (4) Handle `child.on('spawn', ...)` vs `child.on('error', ...)` — ENOENT on jig-not-found arrives as an `error` event on the ChildProcess, not stderr.
24	
25	### SIGTERM + "wait briefly" is not a process lifecycle
26	**Issue:** Crash Recovery § On Harness Exit says "Send SIGTERM to all child agent processes. Wait briefly for graceful shutdown. Exit." This is hand-wavy in three ways: (1) no timeout value, (2) no SIGKILL escalation, (3) no handling of grandchildren. jig spawns claude, claude spawns bash for tool calls, bash runs pnpm, pnpm spawns node. Sending SIGTERM to the jig pid does not kill the tree. Node's `child_process` does not create a process group unless you ask it to, so `kill -TERM -pid` won't work either.
27	**Impact:** On Ctrl-C you get orphaned `claude`, `pnpm install`, `vitest` processes that keep running, keep writing to the worktree, keep consuming tokens, and race with the next harness restart which thinks the workflow was cleanly interrupted. This is the classic "why is my laptop fan on after I killed the harness" bug.
28	**Proposed change:** Spawn children with `detached: true` and a new session (`setsid`) so each child gets its own process group. On shutdown: `process.kill(-child.pid, 'SIGTERM')`, set a timer (concrete: 10s), then `process.kill(-child.pid, 'SIGKILL')`. Also handle the reverse case — if the harness itself is SIGKILLed, the next startup must detect stale processes. Store `{pid, pgid, started_at}` in the `sessions` table and on restart, probe each (kill(pid, 0)) and reap/mark them cancelled before attempting `-c` resume. Add a `harness doctor` command that lists dangling processes.
29	
30	### Claude Code `-c` semantics are load-bearing but unverified
31	**Issue:** The retry loop (Phase 2 § Retry loop), crash recovery (Crash Recovery § On Harness Restart), and the `retry_mode: "continue"` config all assume `claude -c` will reliably resume the previous session in the worktree with the previous context plus new input. The plan never specifies: does `-c` work after an abnormal exit? Does it work after the harness restarts (i.e., across Node processes)? Does it work if another `claude` invocation happened in the same directory in between? Does it work across `jig run <profile>` boundaries, or is session state tied to the exact CLI invocation shape? Does `-c` accept a new `-p` prompt or does it ignore stdin?
32	**Impact:** If `-c` doesn't do what the plan thinks, both the retry loop and crash recovery collapse. The retry loop silently starts a fresh session that doesn't know why it's being invoked; the crash recovery resumes the wrong session or none at all. This is the single biggest unverified assumption in the plan.
33	**Proposed change:** Before writing the retry loop, run empirical tests (document in a `docs/research/continue-semantics.md`): (1) happy-path resume after clean exit, (2) resume after SIGKILL mid-response, (3) resume after hook-exit-2 blocked stop, (4) resume across two Node processes, (5) resume when the worktree has a sibling `claude` session history, (6) resume with a new `-p` prompt on stdin. Only after this should the retry semantics be finalized. Also: define fallback behavior when `-c` fails — start a fresh session with a "you were previously working on X, here's the failure context" preamble. The config option should be `retry_mode: continue | fresh | continue_then_fresh`.
34	
35	### "Retry" is not meaningful inside a single session with a blocking Stop hook
36	**Issue:** Phase 2 § Retry loop says `max_retries: 3` with `retry_mode: continue`. But if the implementer hits a Stop hook that keeps failing (exit 2, stderr fed back), Claude Code itself already loops — the agent fixes, tries to stop, hook blocks, agent fixes again. The harness only sees the session when it finally exits (either cleanly or because the agent gave up). "Retry 3 times" from the harness's perspective means "run a new `claude -c` after the agent declared defeat 3 separate times" — which is a fundamentally different thing from the inner hook-retry loop. The plan conflates these.
37	**Impact:** Users will set `max_retries: 3` expecting 3 attempts to fix the code and get 3 outer-loop re-invocations on top of however many inner hook retries already happened. The meaningful budget (total tool calls? total tokens? inner retries?) is invisible.
38	**Proposed change:** Rename/clarify: `max_outer_retries` (harness re-invokes after the session ends in failure) vs. `max_inner_hook_retries` (Stop hook's own counter — implemented in the hook script, since the hook has no persistent state across calls unless we give it one). Document in the plan that the Stop hook should write a counter to a file in the worktree and give up after N inner retries with exit 0 + a message, letting the session end so the harness can decide what to do. Also define: what does a "retry" prompt look like? The plan says "send the failure context back" but doesn't define what that is — stderr of the failing hook? The last N stream-json events? A manually assembled summary?
39	
40	### Hook exit code semantics need verification and one-sided coverage
41	**Issue:** The Hooks Integration section asserts "exit 2 # blocks the stop, feeds stderr back to Claude." This is asserted as fact but not cited, and the plan doesn't cover exit 1 (generic failure — does Claude Code ignore it? treat it as block? treat it as session-fatal?) or non-Stop hooks (PreToolUse blocking semantics differ from Stop). The plan also doesn't specify the hook's stdin contract (Claude Code passes JSON on stdin to hooks in recent versions) or the stdout contract (JSON responses control decision/block behavior).
42	**Impact:** The entire quality-gate story depends on hook behavior that isn't pinned down. If exit 1 from a hook silently kills the session, the require-passing-checks.sh script as written will cause pipeline deaths instead of retries.
43	**Proposed change:** Add a "Hook Contract" subsection that verifies (needs verification — see Questions) and documents: exact exit code mapping for each hook type (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, SubagentStop, UserPromptSubmit), the stdin JSON schema Claude Code passes to the hook, the stdout JSON schema for structured responses, and what happens when a hook itself crashes (killed by signal, writes malformed JSON, takes >30s). The plan should cite the Claude Code hook docs, not assert behavior.
44	
45	### Worktree creation has unhandled race conditions and failure modes
46	**Issue:** Worktree Management assumes happy-path create/bootstrap/cleanup but doesn't address: (a) two parallel workflows racing on branch names (`harness/add-auth` collision), (b) bootstrap failure mid-run (half-installed node_modules, containers started by `docker compose up -d` that the cleanup doesn't know about), (c) user has uncommitted changes in the source working tree — does `git worktree add` silently inherit them? (it doesn't — worktrees share the repo but each has its own index/HEAD, except that staged changes in the main worktree don't move, but `.gitignore`'d files like `node_modules` and `.env` do NOT appear in the new worktree and must be recreated by bootstrap), (d) cleanup on a worktree whose branch has unmerged commits (`git worktree remove` refuses without `--force`), (e) cleanup racing with a still-running child that holds file handles.
47	**Impact:** First failure scenario corrupts state, second leaves orphan containers, third confuses users ("I had my .env set up, why doesn't the agent see it"), fourth makes `auto_cleanup: true` silently fail, fifth corrupts the next run in the same workflow.
48	**Proposed change:** (1) Branch name collisions: suffix with workflow id (`harness/add-auth-<shortid>`), never reuse. (2) Bootstrap atomicity: treat bootstrap as its own phase in the state machine with its own status; if it fails, mark the workflow `bootstrap_failed` and do NOT auto-cleanup (user needs to inspect). (3) Explicitly document in config guide that bootstrap is responsible for reproducing any gitignored state (env files, secrets, containers) and provide a `.harness/teardown.sh` hook for reverse cleanup (stop containers, etc.), run before `git worktree remove`. (4) Cleanup must `kill` any tracked child pids for that workflow first, then run teardown, then `git worktree remove --force`, then handle the "branch not merged" case (either delete branch or leave it — configurable). (5) Never auto-cleanup a worktree whose branch has unpushed/unmerged commits unless the PR was created successfully.
49	
50	### SQLite schema has no migration story and missing indexes
51	**Issue:** Crash Recovery § State Persistence shows three CREATE TABLE statements. There is no migrations system, no `schema_version` table, no discussion of upgrade paths. There are no indexes declared — but `sessions` will be queried by `workflow_id` and `feature_id` on every state transition and every UI refresh, and `features` by `workflow_id` for the feature board. WAL mode is mentioned ("WAL for concurrent reads") but not how it's enabled, nor the gotchas (sidecar files, fsync on commit, recovery after crash).
52	**Impact:** First schema change breaks every existing deployment with no upgrade path. UI gets slower over time as session logs accumulate. WAL sidecar files left after a crash surprise users.
53	**Proposed change:** (1) Add a `schema_migrations` table and a simple forward-only migration runner (list of SQL files or in-code migrations keyed by version). Run migrations on startup inside a transaction. (2) Declare indexes: `CREATE INDEX idx_features_workflow ON features(workflow_id)`, `CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_workflow ON sessions(workflow_id, feature_id)`, `CREATE INDEX idx_workflows_status ON workflows(status)`. (3) Enable WAL explicitly (`PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL`) and document: (a) WAL requires the DB directory to be writable, (b) `.db-wal` and `.db-shm` sidecar files exist and should not be committed, (c) use `PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL` for the write-heavy orchestration path (still safe with WAL). (4) Wrap every state transition in `db.transaction(() => {...})()` and document that reads during writes are fine but that the UI must use a separate connection for read-only queries (or serialize everything through the writer). (5) Add an `events` append-only log table for debugging state machine transitions — this is worth it from day one.
54	
55	### Prompt template "variable interpolation" is unspecified and is a shell-injection vector if done naively
56	**Issue:** Configuration § Prompt Templates says "Templates use variable interpolation" and shows `{{feature_spec}}` without naming an engine. The plan also doesn't address: escaping (what if `feature_spec` contains `{{something}}`?), missing variables (crash, warn, leave as literal?), nested references, conditionals/loops (is Mustache enough? do we need Handlebars?). The "shell injection" angle is that prompts are assembled into a temp file and piped to stdin — not directly to a shell — so classic injection is not the risk. But the plan also shows `check_command: "claude --usage --output-format json"` as a string that presumably gets shell-exec'd, and the bootstrap `commands: - "pnpm install"` list is similar. Those ARE shell-exec'd and become injection vectors if any variable gets interpolated into them.
57	**Impact:** Template engine choice gets made ad hoc in week one and becomes a compatibility constraint forever. If any config field with interpolation is ever passed to a shell, we get command injection.
58	**Proposed change:** (1) Pick a template engine up front. Recommend a minimal Mustache-style engine (e.g., `mustache` or a 30-line hand-rolled `{{name}}` replacer). Explicitly NO code execution in templates. (2) Define variable resolution rules: undefined variable is a hard error at assembly time (fail the phase, show which variable, which template), never silently empty. (3) Define escaping: no escaping needed for prompt content because it's not going through a shell. (4) The bootstrap `commands` list and `check_command` must be spawned with `spawn(cmd, args, {shell: false})` where possible, and NEVER have any harness-managed variable interpolated into them in v1. If interpolation is needed later, spawn with `shell: false` and pass args as an array. (5) Document the template variable inventory explicitly in the plan (feature_spec, architecture_md, progress_md, git_log_recent, feature_id, workflow_name, ...) so phase authors know what's available.
59	
60	### Feature dependency ordering is in v1.1 but is load-bearing for v1
61	**Issue:** Requirements § Should Have (v1.1) lists "Feature dependency ordering (topological sort of `depends_on`)". But `features.json` already has a `depends_on` field in v1 (File Contract § features.json). If the planner emits features with dependencies and the harness runs them in JSON order, a feature can start while its dependency is still `pending`, the prompt won't include the dependency's code because it doesn't exist yet, and the implementer will reinvent or block. The state machine also needs to handle "feature A failed → features that depend on A should auto-block."
62	**Impact:** v1 either ignores the `depends_on` field (misleading — users will set it and wonder why ordering is wrong) or crashes when it's non-empty. Dependency cascade-blocking is absent.
63	**Proposed change:** Move topological sort into v1. It's a 20-line function and it's cheaper to build now than to retrofit. At minimum in v1: (a) topo-sort features before implementation phase, (b) on feature failure, mark all transitively dependent features as `blocked` with `blocked_reason: "dependency feat-NNN failed"`, (c) reject cycles at planning-validation time with a clear error. Advanced parallel scheduling of independent features can still be v1.1.
64	
65	### Heartbeat "output activity in last N minutes" is the wrong signal during long tool calls
66	**Issue:** Process Management § Heartbeat says "Has stdout emitted anything in the last N minutes? (generous default — maybe 5 minutes)." But stream-json emits nothing while a single `Bash` tool call is running — e.g., a 12-minute integration test suite, a long `pnpm install`, a docker build. 5 minutes will false-positive on any real build. The plan acknowledges this ("a process might legitimately be doing a long build") but then still proposes the timer.
67	**Impact:** Harness kills legitimate long-running work, or the user sets N so high that the heartbeat is useless.
68	**Proposed change:** Distinguish two signals: (1) **Liveness** — PID still running AND, if the session is in a tool call, the tool's child process is still running. Use the stream-json tool_use events to track "we're currently inside tool X, started at T." Never alert on stdout silence while a tool is executing. (2) **Stream activity** — only apply a stdout-silence threshold when NOT inside a tool_use. (3) Make the "kill after silence" action user-confirmed by default — heartbeat surfaces a warning in the UI, does not kill. The user clicks "kill and retry" if they judge it stuck. This matches the plan's "user can judge whether the agent is stuck" intent but actually implements it.
69	
70	### Token usage parsing from stream-json is underspecified
71	**Issue:** Usage Tracking shows a `{input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens}` shape as the capture target, but doesn't specify which stream-json event carries the usage numbers, whether it's per-turn or cumulative, whether it's updated continuously or only at session end, or how to reconcile when a session is `-c`-resumed (is the usage for the original + resumed, or only the resumed slice?).
72	**Impact:** Token reporting is either wrong (double-counting resumed sessions) or silently empty (we're parsing the wrong event).
73	**Proposed change:** (needs verification — see Questions). Specify: which event type(s) carry usage, accumulation semantics, and how resumed sessions are linked in the DB. Proposed DB shape: add `parent_session_id TEXT REFERENCES sessions(id)` so a resumed session can chain; per-session usage is the delta from that session only; per-feature usage is the sum across the session chain.
74	
75	### Config validation via ajv + YAML has known footguns the plan doesn't address
76	**Issue:** Tech Stack names `yaml + ajv` but Configuration § doesn't address: (a) YAML number-like strings (the infamous `version: 1.10` parsing as 1.1), (b) YAML boolean surprise (`on`, `off`, `yes`, `no` in YAML 1.1 vs 1.2 — the `yaml` package defaults to 1.2 but still), (c) ajv error messages are cryptic by default ("must match exactly one schema in oneOf"), (d) schema evolution across versions, (e) unknown-key behavior (typos in config keys silently ignored unless `additionalProperties: false` is set everywhere).
77	**Impact:** First user to typo a config key loses a day wondering why their setting doesn't work. First user with a `version: 1.10` release in config gets 1.1. Every validation error is unreadable.
78	**Proposed change:** (1) Require `version: "1"` at the top of `.harness.yml` (quoted string) and validate against it — gives us schema evolution from day one. (2) Set `additionalProperties: false` on every object schema. (3) Use `ajv-errors` + `better-ajv-errors` (or a custom formatter) to produce errors like `worktrees.base_dir: expected string, got null (line 12)`. (4) Document the YAML 1.2 assumption and add a lint-time warning for bare `on/off/yes/no` values (they're strings in 1.2 but users expect them to be booleans). (5) Validate file paths at load time (templates exist, schemas exist) — not at phase-run time.
79	
80	### GitHub integration "octokit + gh fallback" glosses over auth and the actual fallback trigger
81	**Issue:** Tech Stack says "Octokit + gh CLI fallback" and the dashboard section mentions "If auto-PR fails or is disabled, user clicks to create via `gh` CLI." This conflates two different things: (a) using gh as a fallback when octokit fails, (b) using gh when the user clicks a button. It also doesn't address auth: does the harness read `GITHUB_TOKEN` from env? from `gh auth token`? from `~/.config/gh/hosts.yml`? What about GHES? What about rate limits? What about the PR creation happening from a detached worktree branch that isn't pushed yet (need to `git push` first — who does that)?
82	**Impact:** Auth story is undefined, will ship broken on first non-laptop user. Branch-push-before-PR is a separate step that's invisible in the plan.
83	**Proposed change:** (1) Auth resolution order: `GITHUB_TOKEN` env, then shell out to `gh auth token`, then error with clear guidance. Document this explicitly. (2) Separate the two flows: "auto-PR" uses octokit by default (falls back to gh if octokit errors with a well-defined set of auth/network errors); "manual PR button" always uses gh (because the user has gh configured and we know it works). (3) Explicit pre-PR step: `git push -u origin <branch>` must happen before PR creation; surface push failures (auth, protected branch) distinctly from PR-creation failures. (4) Defer GHES support to v2 and say so.
84	
85	### "Workflow in_progress" recovery doesn't specify what phase to resume in
86	**Issue:** Crash Recovery § On Harness Restart says "For any workflow that was `in_progress`: check if the worktree exists... attempt `claude -c`... if that fails, start a fresh session for the interrupted phase." But the plan's state model stores `status` on workflows and features, and `current_phase` on features. It doesn't store which session was active, which subagent, whether a hook was in the middle of blocking a stop, or whether the agent was mid-commit. A crash during a commit leaves a dirty worktree the next session will choke on.
87	**Impact:** Resume lands in an inconsistent state and the fresh session gets confused by uncommitted changes from the crashed session.
88	**Proposed change:** (1) On every stream-json event of interest (assistant turn start, tool call start/end, hook fire), update `sessions.last_event_at` and `sessions.last_event_type` so restart knows roughly where we were. (2) On restart, before attempting `-c`, inspect the worktree: if `git status` is dirty, either (a) auto-stash with a `harness-crash-stash-<timestamp>` name and surface it in the UI, or (b) commit as WIP with a `[harness-crash-wip]` prefix — configurable. Never run `-c` against a dirty worktree without preserving the work first. (3) For features in `review` state when the crash happened, the resume must know whether the review phase had already written files (check `reviews/feature-N/` for partial files and delete them so the re-run starts clean).
89	
90	### `--dangerously-skip-permissions` as default is a policy decision the plan makes silently
91	**Issue:** Phase 2 § Permission mode says `--dangerously-skip-permissions` is the default (configurable per phase). This is a real decision with real blast radius — the agent can `rm -rf`, modify files outside the worktree (worktree isolation doesn't stop `~/.ssh/config` writes), and there's nothing stopping a prompt injection in the planning spec from escalating. The plan doesn't discuss the PreToolUse hook as a mitigation (the table mentions "Dangerous commands blocked | PreToolUse hook" but doesn't follow through).
92	**Impact:** First prompt-injection incident destroys something outside the worktree and the harness is blamed.
93	**Proposed change:** (1) Keep `bypassPermissions` as the *default for implementer*, but make the harness install (or require) a PreToolUse hook that denies writes/reads outside the worktree path and denies a curated list of dangerous Bash patterns (`rm -rf /`, `chmod -R 777`, writes to `~/.ssh`, `~/.aws`, `~/.config`, `/etc/`, etc.). Make this hook ship as part of `harness init` scaffolding so every new project gets it. (2) Document the threat model explicitly in the plan: "The planner spec is untrusted input. The harness must not assume the implementer won't try to escape the worktree." (3) Offer a config flag `safety_mode: strict | default | yolo` that toggles the hook set.
94	
95	## Questions (need user input)
96	
97	- Needs verification: What is the exact framing of Claude Code's `--output-format stream-json` output? NDJSON (one JSON object per line, `\n`-delimited)? Concatenated objects with no delimiter? Some event types multi-line? The parser design depends on this. Can you point me at the stream-json docs or a captured transcript I can inspect?
98	
99	- Needs verification: Exact semantics of `claude -c`. Specifically: (a) does it work across separate Node processes (harness restart)? (b) does it work after SIGKILL of the prior `claude` process? (c) does it accept a new prompt on stdin, or does it ignore stdin and just continue from the last state? (d) is "the last session in this directory" stored where — `.claude/` in the worktree, or user-global? (e) does `jig run <profile> -- -c ...` preserve session state if the profile changes between calls? This is the single most load-bearing assumption in the plan.
100	
101	- Needs verification: jig-specific gotchas. What does `jig run <profile> --` actually do with cwd (does it honor the caller's cwd or does jig cd somewhere)? Does jig fork its own child or exec? Does jig propagate signals to the claude child? Is there a known "spawn jig from Node" pitfall (shell alias vs. binary on PATH, `.tool-versions` resolution, profile-not-found exit codes)?
102	
103	- Needs verification: Hook exit code semantics for each hook type. The plan asserts `exit 2` on a Stop hook blocks and feeds stderr back. Is that documented? What does exit 1 do? What does exit on signal do (hook killed)? Does Claude Code pass JSON to the hook on stdin, and does structured JSON output from the hook override exit codes? Are hooks invoked per-session or per-matcher-match, and can a Stop hook see the session transcript?
104	
105	- Needs verification: Does Claude Code emit token usage in stream-json incrementally (per turn) or only in a terminal event? If per-turn, is it cumulative or delta? How does `-c` resume affect the usage numbers emitted — do we see only the new slice, or the re-hydrated totals?
106	
107	- Should the harness own the PreToolUse safety hook, or is that delegated to the project's own `.claude/hooks`? If the harness owns it, does `harness init` scaffold it and how do we version-upgrade it when users have customized it?
108	
109	- What's the intended relationship between the harness's retry loop and any retry logic inside the Stop hook itself? Is the hook expected to be stateless (retry forever until the agent gives up) or stateful (maintain a counter file and exit 0 after N)? This determines whether "max_retries: 3" in config means inner or outer.
110	
111	- For parallel workflows: is the intent that multiple workflows can target the same base branch concurrently, or is there a lock? If concurrent, how do we handle the case where workflow A's PR gets merged while workflow B is still running off a now-stale base?
112	
113	- The plan mentions `lazyworktree` as a possible cleanup tool — is that a real tool I should integrate against, or a placeholder? Do you have a preferred cleanup contract (stdin/stdout? argv? exit codes?) for the `cleanup_tool: custom` case?
114	
115	- Session logs: `retain_stream_json: true` captures everything. What's the retention policy — forever? Rotate after N days/MB? Who deletes? This matters for the schema migration and disk-space story.
116	
117	- Is the v1 dashboard single-user (localhost, no auth) or multi-user? The plan says "runs locally" but also mentions "browser push" notifications. If it's ever remote, we need an auth story in v1, not v2.
118

1	# Yoke — Build Progress
2	
3	## feat-process-mgr-heartbeat (2026-04-13)
4	
5	Implemented `src/server/process/heartbeat.ts` — the two-signal heartbeat for a running child process. The `Heartbeat` class owns a single `setInterval` (tick = `liveness_interval_s` from `ResolvedConfig`). On each tick, it calls `isRateLimited()` fresh (AC-5/RC-2 — suppression state is never cached); if not suppressed, it runs two independent probes: (1) the liveness probe calls `process.kill(pid, 0)` — signal 0 delivers no signal but causes ESRCH if the PID is gone — and emits one `stream.system_notice{subtype:'liveness_stale'}` warning on the first ESRCH (subsequent ESRCH is muted since the session is ending); (2) the stream-activity watchdog computes `Date.now() - lastStdoutAt` and emits one `stream.system_notice{subtype:'stream_idle'}` if idle time exceeds `activity_timeout_s`; the watchdog is reset by `notifyStdoutLine()` so each new idle period can warn once. Neither probe sends SIGTERM or SIGKILL (RC-1). `stop()` calls `clearInterval` (RC-3). Added `liveness_interval_s?: number` (default 30 s) to `Heartbeat` in `src/shared/types/config.ts` and to the schema `$def` in `docs/design/schemas/yoke-config.schema.json` — required to satisfy AC-4 (interval from `ResolvedConfig`, not hard-coded). 22 tests in `tests/process/heartbeat.test.ts` using vitest fake timers and `vi.spyOn(process, 'kill')` cover all 5 acceptance criteria and 3 review criteria; all 392 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
6	
7	## feat-process-mgr-ndjson (2026-04-13)
8	
9	Implemented `src/server/process/stream-json.ts` — the NDJSON line-buffered stream-json parser that
10	consumes Claude Code `--output-format stream-json` stdout and emits six typed events: `stream.text`,
11	`stream.thinking`, `stream.tool_use`, `stream.tool_result`, `stream.usage`, `stream.system_notice`,
12	plus `rate_limit_detected` and `parse_error`. The parser is a `StreamJsonParser extends EventEmitter`
13	with two input methods: `feed(line: string)` for complete lines (used by the JigProcessManager
14	readline path) and `feedChunk(data: string)` for arbitrary byte chunks with a `flush()` to drain the
15	partial-line buffer. Splitting is on LF (`\n`) only — matches the empirical finding that Claude Code
16	never emits CRLF. Two streaming modes are handled transparently: in default mode, complete `assistant`
17	events yield `stream.text`/`stream.thinking` with `final:true`; in `--include-partial-messages` mode,
18	`stream_event content_block_delta` events yield deltas with `final:false` and `stream_event
19	content_block_stop` emits an empty-delta `final:true` event, while the duplicate `assistant` text/
20	thinking events are suppressed. `stream.tool_use` is always emitted from the complete `assistant`
21	event. Rate-limit detection fires when `rate_limit_event.rate_limit_info.status !== "allowed"` with
22	`resetAt` extracted from `resetsAt`. Malformed JSON lines emit `parse_error` (the Pipeline Engine is
23	responsible for incrementing `sessions.status_flags.parse_errors` in SQLite on receipt). 39 tests in
24	`tests/process/stream-json.test.ts` covering all 6 acceptance criteria and all 4 review criteria
25	against two empirically captured fixture files (`capture-default-mode.jsonl` = research capture-1,
26	`capture-partial-messages.jsonl` = research capture-4); all 370 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.
27	
28	## feat-process-mgr-live (2026-04-13)
29	
30	Implemented `src/server/process/manager.ts` and `src/server/process/jig-manager.ts` — the
31	`ProcessManager` interface and its production implementation. `manager.ts` exports `ProcessError`
32	(discriminated kind: `epipe | spawn_failed | stdin_error`), `SpawnOpts` (command + args from
33	ResolvedConfig only, no hard-coded strings), `SpawnHandle` (typed event overloads: `stdout_line`,
34	`stderr_data`, `stderr_cap_reached`, `exit`, `error`; plus `isAlive()` and `cancel()`), and
35	`ProcessManager` (single `spawn(opts)` method — no JigProcessManager-specific leakage). `jig-manager.ts`
36	exports only `JigProcessManager`; the internal `JigSpawnHandle extends EventEmitter` is not exported.
37	Key mechanics: `detached:true` gives the child its own process group (`pgid === pid`); `child.unref()`
38	allows the event loop to exit without waiting (zombie reap documented in a code comment); EPIPE is
39	caught as a named failure class in the stdin `error` handler (not a generic catch) and re-raised as
40	`ProcessError{ kind: 'epipe' }`; stderr is capped at 64 KB in the `data` stream handler (slice-before-emit,
41	never accumulate all bytes first); `stderr_cap_reached` is emitted once; `cancel()` sends SIGTERM to the
42	entire process group (`process.kill(-pgid, 'SIGTERM')`), waits `gracePeriodMs` (default 10 000 ms),
43	then escalates to SIGKILL. The SIGTERM→SIGKILL escalation test uses a readiness protocol (child writes
44	`"ready\n"` after registering its handler) to eliminate the signal-before-handler-registration race. 22
45	integration tests in `tests/process/jig-manager.test.ts`; all 331 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.

47	## feat-state-machine-tests (2026-04-12)
48	
49	Added `tests/state-machine/transitions-assertions.test.ts` — the full 19-assertion
50	unit test suite for the state machine, mapped 1-to-1 to the §Test assertion list in
51	`docs/design/state-machine-transitions.md`. All 19 named assertions are present and
52	none are skipped. Assertion 1 is the completeness test: it defines the 33 documented
53	non-abandoned pairs explicitly and verifies each returns a defined result from
54	`transition()`, plus checks that all 27 events are handled by `abandoned` and that
55	undocumented pairs return `undefined` (no phantom rows). Assertions 2–19 cover valid
56	to-states, guard semantics (`deps_satisfied` requires `complete` deps, `complete`
57	requires all four conditions), bootstrap-failed non-auto-transition, phase advance
58	atomicity, goto/max_revisits, stop-and-ask, user_cancel universality (with the
59	bootstrapping gap documented), cascade side effects, logging side effects,
60	idempotency of the crash recovery path (referential equality of pure function
61	outputs), and stage-level events as workflow-level concerns. 64 new tests; total
62	309 tests pass; all tests run in ~10ms (pure data-structure assertions, no I/O).
63	
64	## feat-db-setup (2026-04-12)
65	
66	Implemented `src/server/storage/db.ts` — the `better-sqlite3` connection pool
67	that forms the storage foundation for Yoke. `openDbPool(dbPath)` opens a single
68	writer connection with all four PRAGMAs (`busy_timeout=5000` first, then
69	`journal_mode=WAL`, `synchronous=NORMAL`, `foreign_keys=ON`), bootstraps the
70	`schema_migrations` table with `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` (idempotent on
71	re-open), then opens a separate read-only reader connection with `{
72	readonly: true }` (enforced at the connection level, not by convention) and
73	applies `busy_timeout` and `foreign_keys` to it. The module exports only the
74	`openDbPool` factory and the `DbPool` interface — no module-level singleton.
75	Added `better-sqlite3` + `@types/better-sqlite3` to package.json. 17
76	integration tests in `tests/storage/db.test.ts` cover every acceptance
77	criterion; all 48 tests pass, `tsc --noEmit` clean.

80	
81	Implemented `src/server/storage/migrations/0001_core_tables.sql` — DDL for all
82	seven core tables (workflows, items, sessions, events, artifact_writes,
83	pending_attention, prepost_runs) with all indexes and FK semantics exactly as
84	specified in `docs/design/schemas/sqlite-schema.sql`. The partial index
85	`idx_pending_attention_open` on `pending_attention(workflow_id) WHERE
86	acknowledged_at IS NULL` is present and verified by EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN in tests.
87	All FK ON DELETE CASCADE relationships cascade correctly from workflow deletion;
88	SET NULL is applied where specified (sessions.item_id, events.item_id,
89	events.session_id, prepost_runs.session_id, prepost_runs.item_id). The
90	`prepost_runs.when_phase` CHECK constraint (pre|post) is enforced by SQLite.
91	`src/server/storage/migrate.ts` provides the `applyMigrations(writer,
92	migrationsDir)` forward-only runner: skips already-applied versions, wraps each
93	migration in a transaction, records the version in schema_migrations on success.
94	42 new integration tests in `tests/storage/schema.test.ts`; all 90 tests pass,
95	`tsc --noEmit` clean.

97	## feat-db-migrations (2026-04-12)
98	
99	Completed the forward-only migrations runner in `src/server/storage/migrate.ts`.
100	The existing `applyMigrations` skeleton (from feat-db-schema) already handled
101	discovery, transaction wrapping, and idempotent skip; this session added the two
102	missing regression guards: (1) a pre-loop RC-3 check that throws if
103	`schema_migrations` contains a version higher than the highest known file — a
104	forward-only invariant protecting against deleted or misplaced migration
105	directories; (2) a per-file AC-3 check that throws when an unapplied file has a
106	version lower than the highest already-applied version, naming both version
107	numbers in the error message. The sort comparator was made explicitly numeric
108	(`parseInt` subtraction) rather than lexicographic to satisfy RC-2. A dedicated
109	`tests/storage/migrate.test.ts` file (18 integration tests) covers all five
110	acceptance criteria (AC-1..AC-5) and the three relevant review criteria
111	(RC-2..RC-4); all 108 tests pass, `tsc --noEmit` clean.
112	
113	## feat-db-transaction (2026-04-12)
114	
115	Added `pool.transaction<T>(fn)` to the `DbPool` interface and implemented it in
116	`openDbPool`. The implementation is a single line: `writer.transaction(fn)(writer)`.
117	`better-sqlite3` handles BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK internally; if `fn` throws, it rolls
118	back and re-throws the original exception without wrapping. There is no catch block
119	in the wrapper — RC-1 is satisfied structurally, not by convention. The wrapper is
120	injected via `DbPool` (RC-3); no module-level transaction state exists. `fn` receives
121	`Database.Database` and returns `T`; the outer call returns `T` (AC-5, verified by
122	`tsc --noEmit`). 15 integration tests in `tests/storage/transaction.test.ts` cover
123	AC-1 (two-table write atomic after simulated mid-transaction crash, successful
124	commit, rollback + re-use), AC-2 (same Error instance re-thrown, no message
125	augmentation, non-Error throws pass through), AC-3 (reader cannot see uncommitted
126	WAL writes; sees them post-commit; sees nothing after rollback), and AC-5 (typed
127	return value, fn receives the writer connection). All 123 tests pass;
128	`tsc --noEmit` clean.
129	
130	## feat-state-machine (2026-04-12)
131	
132	Implemented the state machine module in full under `src/server/state-machine/`.
133	Three new TypeScript modules: `states.ts` (11-element `State` and 27-element
134	`Event` union literal types), `transitions.ts` (the `TRANSITIONS` const typed as
135	`{ [S in State]: Partial<Record<Event, TransitionResult>> }` — the mapped-type
136	key forces a compile-time error when a new State is added without a corresponding
137	entry; the `abandoned` row is typed as `satisfies Record<Event, TransitionResult>`
138	so adding a new Event also errors; every row is runtime-frozen via
139	`Object.freeze`; `transition()` is a pure lookup with no I/O), and
140	`classifier.ts` (`classify(stderr, parseState): FailureClass` — tags
141	rate-limit/overload patterns as `transient`, auth/permission patterns as
142	`permanent`, content-policy patterns as `policy`, and all other patterns as
143	`unknown`; parse state — parse_errors count + last event type — is a required
144	input per RC-3 and affects classification when the stream was wholly
145	unparseable). The `session_fail` TRANSITIONS entry has a conditional result with
146	three guarded outcomes: `classifier=transient → awaiting_retry`,
147	`classifier=permanent → awaiting_user`, `classifier=unknown → awaiting_user`,
148	so `unknown` can never reach a retry path. One known gap filed in handoff.json:
149	`(bootstrapping, user_cancel)` is absent from state-machine-transitions.md;
150	the test explicitly documents this as "not in the doc." 122 new tests across
151	`tests/state-machine/transitions.test.ts` (49) and `tests/state-machine/classifier.test.ts` (73);
152	all 245 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.

154	## feat-config-loader (2026-04-12)

156	Implemented the synchronous `.yoke.yml` config loader in full. The feature

158	and ResolvedConfig type definitions, derived from the JSON schema), `src/server/

1	-- Migration 0001 — core tables
2	-- Defines all seven operational tables with indexes and FK semantics.
3	-- schema_migrations is bootstrapped by openDbPool before this runs.
4	-- PRAGMAs (WAL, synchronous, foreign_keys, busy_timeout) are set by
5	-- openDbPool on every connection open, not in migration files.
6	
7	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
8	-- workflows — one row per named workflow run.
9	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
10	CREATE TABLE workflows (
11	  id              TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
12	  name            TEXT NOT NULL,
13	  spec            TEXT NOT NULL,
14	  pipeline        TEXT NOT NULL,
15	  config          TEXT NOT NULL,
16	  status          TEXT NOT NULL,
17	  current_stage   TEXT,
18	  worktree_path   TEXT,
19	  branch_name     TEXT,
20	  recovery_state  TEXT,
21	  created_at      TEXT NOT NULL,
22	  updated_at      TEXT NOT NULL
23	);
24	CREATE INDEX idx_workflows_status ON workflows(status);
25	CREATE INDEX idx_workflows_created_at ON workflows(created_at DESC);
26	
27	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
28	-- items — canonical item store. Item data is opaque to the harness.
29	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
30	CREATE TABLE items (
31	  id                          TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
32	  workflow_id                 TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workflows(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
33	  stage_id                    TEXT NOT NULL,
34	  data                        TEXT NOT NULL,
35	  status                      TEXT NOT NULL,
36	  current_phase               TEXT,
37	  depends_on                  TEXT,
38	  retry_count                 INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
39	  retry_window_start          TEXT,
40	  created_by_session_id       TEXT,
41	  last_updated_by_session_id  TEXT,
42	  blocked_reason              TEXT,
43	  updated_at                  TEXT NOT NULL
44	);
45	CREATE INDEX idx_items_workflow ON items(workflow_id);
46	CREATE INDEX idx_items_status ON items(workflow_id, status);
47	CREATE INDEX idx_items_stage ON items(workflow_id, stage_id);
48	
49	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
50	-- sessions — one row per spawned agent session.
51	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
52	CREATE TABLE sessions (
53	  id                          TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
54	  workflow_id                 TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workflows(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
55	  item_id                     TEXT REFERENCES items(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
56	  parent_session_id           TEXT REFERENCES sessions(id),
57	  stage                       TEXT NOT NULL,
58	  phase                       TEXT NOT NULL,
59	  agent_profile               TEXT NOT NULL,
60	  pid                         INTEGER,
61	  pgid                        INTEGER,
62	  started_at                  TEXT NOT NULL,
63	  ended_at                    TEXT,
64	  exit_code                   INTEGER,
65	  input_tokens                INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
66	  output_tokens               INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
67	  cache_creation_input_tokens INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
68	  cache_read_input_tokens     INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
69	  raw_usage                   TEXT,
70	  session_log_path            TEXT,
71	  status                      TEXT NOT NULL,
72	  status_flags                TEXT,
73	  last_event_at               TEXT,
74	  last_event_type             TEXT
75	);
76	CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_workflow ON sessions(workflow_id, item_id);
77	CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_item ON sessions(item_id);
78	CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_parent ON sessions(parent_session_id);
79	
80	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
81	-- events — append-only state-machine + harness debug trace.
82	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
83	CREATE TABLE events (
84	  id          INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
85	  ts          TEXT NOT NULL,
86	  workflow_id TEXT REFERENCES workflows(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
87	  item_id     TEXT REFERENCES items(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
88	  session_id  TEXT REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE SET NULL,
89	  stage       TEXT,
90	  phase       TEXT,

96	);
97	CREATE INDEX idx_events_workflow ON events(workflow_id, ts);
98	CREATE INDEX idx_events_session ON events(session_id, ts);
99	CREATE INDEX idx_events_type ON events(event_type);
100	
101	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
102	-- artifact_writes — provenance trail for every file written by a session.
103	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
104	CREATE TABLE artifact_writes (
105	  id            INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
106	  session_id    TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES sessions(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
107	  artifact_path TEXT NOT NULL,

110	);
111	CREATE INDEX idx_artifact_writes_session ON artifact_writes(session_id);
112	CREATE INDEX idx_artifact_writes_path ON artifact_writes(artifact_path);
113	
114	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

116	-- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
117	CREATE TABLE pending_attention (

149	CREATE INDEX idx_prepost_session ON prepost_runs(session_id);

246	## State Machine (new in draft3)
247	
248	### States
249	
250	| State | Meaning |
251	|---|---|
252	| `pending` | feature not yet started |
253	| `ready` | dependencies complete, eligible to start |
254	| `bootstrapping` | worktree bootstrap running |
255	| `bootstrap_failed` | terminal until user action; no auto-cleanup |
256	| `in_progress` | implementer session active |
257	| `awaiting_retry` | between failed attempt and next attempt (backoff window) |
258	| `review` | reviewer orchestrator running |
259	| `review_invalid` | reviewer output failed schema validation; re-run |
260	| `rate_limited` | Claude Code signaled rate limit; waiting with backoff |
261	| `awaiting_user` | all retries exhausted or policy requires human decision |
262	| `blocked` | manual/dependency block; user-reachable with "retry with notes" |
263	| `complete` | all reviews passed |
264	| `abandoned` | user cancelled or workflow aborted |
265	
266	### Transition table
267	
268	Implemented as a TypeScript `const` with a unit test asserting every `(state, event)` pair has a defined transition. Draft3 ships the full table as `src/server/state-machine/transitions.ts`; critical rows summarized here:
269	
270	```
271	(pending, deps_satisfied)             → ready
272	(ready, implement_phase_start)        → bootstrapping → in_progress
273	(in_progress, session_ok + manifest)  → review
274	(in_progress, session_fail + budget)  → awaiting_retry
275	(in_progress, rate_limit_detected)    → rate_limited
276	(awaiting_retry, backoff_done)        → in_progress
277	(rate_limited, probe_ok)              → in_progress
278	(review, all_verdicts_pass)           → complete
279	(review, any_verdict_fail + budget)   → in_progress (fresh)
280	(review, verdict_missing_or_invalid)  → review_invalid
281	(review_invalid, rerun)               → review
282	(*, retries_exhausted)                → awaiting_user
283	(awaiting_user, user_retry)           → in_progress
284	(awaiting_user, user_block)           → blocked
285	(blocked, user_unblock_with_notes)    → in_progress (fresh + handoff)
286	(*, user_cancel)                      → abandoned
287	```
288	
289	Every feature failure runs through the **failure classifier**: `transient | permanent | policy | unknown`. Transient uses exponential backoff in a retry window, not a monotonic counter. Default unknown → `awaiting_user`. [D07]
290	
291	### Dependency cascading
292	
293	Topological sort is v1. On `in_progress → awaiting_user` or `blocked`, the harness marks all transitive dependents with `blocked` + `blocked_reason: "dependency <feat-id> <status>"`. [D08]
294	
295	---
296	
297	## File Contract
298	
299	### features.json (in worktree — **read-only projection**)
300	
301	The canonical feature store is the SQLite `features` table. `features.json` in the worktree is a snapshot written by the harness before each phase and deleted after. The implementer may **read** it but not write it.
302	
303	Status updates from the implementer go through a narrow channel: the implementer appends lines to `.yoke/status-updates.jsonl`. Harness parses after session end and updates SQLite. The harness post-phase diff check rejects any non-whitespace change to `features.json` itself. [D10]
304	
305	```json
306	{

314	      "priority": 1,

320	      ],

324	        "Input sanitization on all form fields"
325	      ],

332	    }

337	### .yoke/status-updates.jsonl (implementer → harness)
338	
339	```jsonl
340	{"feature_id":"feat-001","status":"review","commit":"abc1234","ts":"..."}
341	```
342	
343	### handoff.json (structured cross-phase channel)
344	
345	Per-workflow, append-only. Solves the "progress.md is free-form and reviewers need context" leak. [D13]
346	
347	```json
348	{
349	  "feature_id": "feat-001",
350	  "entries": [
351	    {
352	      "phase": "implement",
353	      "attempt": 1,
354	      "session_id": "sess-abc",
355	      "ts": "...",
356	      "intended_files": ["src/auth/login.ts", "src/auth/login.test.ts"],
357	      "deferred_criteria": [],
358	      "known_risks": ["bcrypt cost factor hardcoded at 10"],
359	      "retry_history": [],
360	      "reviewer_notes_seen": [],
361	      "user_injected_context": null
362	    }
363	  ]
364	}
365	```
366	
367	User "inject context" entries queue here as `user_injected_context` and are consumed by the next session of that feature. [D43]
368	
369	### progress.md
370	
371	Free-form narrative. Not a contract. Exists alongside handoff.json.
372	
373	### reviews/feature-N/<angle>.json
374	
375	Each reviewer subagent writes its own file. Schema is fixed:
376	
377	```json
378	{
379	  "feature_id": "feat-001",
380	  "reviewer": "security",
381	  "reviewed_commit": "abc1234",
382	  "verdict": "fail",
383	  "acceptance_criteria_verdicts": [
384	    {"criterion": "Login form accepts email and password", "pass": true, "notes": ""}
385	  ],
386	  "review_criteria_verdicts": [
387	    {"criterion": "No credentials stored in plaintext", "pass": true, "notes": ""}
388	  ],
389	  "additional_issues": [
390	    {"severity": "high", "category": "security", "description": "...", "file": "...", "suggestion": "..."}
391	  ]
392	}
393	```
394	
395	Every planner-defined criterion must appear in its verdicts array. Harness rejects incomplete reviews as `review_invalid`.
396	
397	---
398	
399	## Protocol Layer — stream-json parsing + WebSocket
400	
401	### stream-json parsing [D15, D16]
402	
403	**Framing:** NDJSON (one JSON object per line, `\n`-delimited). ⚠ empirical verification required in Phase γ research (see runbook).
404	
405	**Parser:**
406	- Line-buffered reader: accumulate bytes in a `Buffer`, split on `\n`, parse complete lines, carry the trailing partial forward.
407	- Use `readline.createInterface({ input: child.stdout, crlfDelay: Infinity })` or a hand-rolled splitter. **Never** `JSON.parse` raw `data` chunks.
408	- Parse failure → log + skip + set `sessions.status_flags.parse_errors += 1`, do not crash pipeline.
409	- Line > 16 MB → truncate, mark session tainted with an `event`.
410	- stderr is a separate stream — never mixed into the JSON parser.
411	
412	**Token usage:** extracted from stream-json events, written to `sessions` columns:
413	- `input_tokens`, `output_tokens`, `cache_creation_input_tokens`, `cache_read_input_tokens`, `raw_usage TEXT`
414	- Resumed sessions chain via `parent_session_id`; per-session = delta, per-feature = sum across chain.
415	- Exact event carrying usage → **to verify in Phase γ research**.
416	
417	### WebSocket envelope [D17]
418	
419	**Server → client:**
420	```ts
421	interface ServerFrame {
422	  v: 1;
423	  type: ServerFrameType;
424	  workflowId: string;
425	  sessionId?: string;
426	  seq: number;        // monotonic per session
427	  ts: string;
428	  payload: unknown;
429	}
430	```
431	Types: `hello`, `workflow.snapshot`, `workflow.update`, `feature.update`, `session.started`, `session.ended`, `stream.chunk`, `stream.tool_use`, `stream.tool_result`, `stream.text`, `stream.thinking`, `stream.usage`, `stream.system_notice`, `notice`, `error`, `pong`, `backfill.truncated`, `workflow.index.update`.
432	
433	**Client → server:**
434	```ts
435	interface ClientFrame {
436	  v: 1;
437	  type: 'subscribe' | 'unsubscribe' | 'control' | 'ack' | 'ping';
438	  id: string;    // client-generated commandId (idempotency)
439	  payload: unknown;
440	}
441	```
442	
443	On connect, server emits `hello { v: 1, serverVersion }`. Client refuses mismatched version instead of silent garbage rendering.
444	
445	### Backfill on reconnect [D19]
446	
447	Client sends `subscribe { workflowId, sinceSeq? }`. Server replies with `workflow.snapshot` + bounded backfill from the Session Log Store, then switches to live. If `sinceSeq` is older than retained buffer, server returns `backfill.truncated` with an HTTP URL for paged fetch. Client dedupes by `(sessionId, seq)`.
448	
449	### Subscription model [D20]
450	
451	Single multiplexed socket per client. Explicit `subscribe`/`unsubscribe` by workflowId. Server only sends subscribed workflows + a lightweight `workflow.index.update` for the sidebar list. Cap: 4 concurrent streaming subscriptions; extras degrade to polled snapshots.
452	
453	---
454	
455	## Client Render Model [D18]
456	
457	The client reduces wire frames into normalized events, distinct from the wire format:
458	
459	| Event | Behavior |
460	|---|---|
461	| `TextBlock` | Mutable; deltas accumulate until `content_block_stop`, then frozen. Rendered as a stable component with mutable text ref. |
462	| `ToolCall` | Tool name, input JSON, status `pending → running → ok/error`, linked to `tool_result` by `tool_use_id`. Collapsible. |
463	| `ThinkingBlock` | Collapsed by default, muted treatment. |
464	| `SystemNotice` | Session start/end, retries, hook failures, rate-limit detection, harness state transitions. Left-rule accent, severity colored. [D49 feeds into this] |
465	| `UsageUpdate` | Not rendered inline; feeds the HUD. |
466	
467	Virtualization: `@tanstack/react-virtual` with variable-size + measure-on-mount. Follow-tail within N pixels of bottom; detach on upscroll; "Jump to latest" pill. Text deltas buffered in 16ms rAF flush. Hard cap 10k events in-memory; older events paged from Session Log Store. Persistent scroll position across tab switches. [D41]
468	
469	---
470	
471	## Hooks Integration [D55]
472	
473	Yoke does **not** own a hook directory. Claude Code's hooks are the project's own — installed wherever Claude Code expects them (e.g., `.claude/hooks/`), managed however the user manages them (plain files, jig profiles, etc.). Yoke's job is to *interoperate* with whatever the user configures, not to install or replace it.
474	
475	### What Yoke provides
476	
477	1. **Example templates** in `docs/templates/hooks/`. Users copy these into their Claude hook directory if they want a quick-start. `yoke init` may offer to scaffold them on opt-in; it never overwrites existing hooks.
478	2. **A conventional data channel** — any Claude Stop hook that writes `.yoke/last-check.json` in the worktree will have that manifest surfaced in the dashboard and (if the user declares it) validated via `post:` commands. The manifest shape is documented; the channel is **optional** — Yoke does not require it.
479	3. **Acceptance rules** for a phase (see Principle #2): agent exit clean + all `post:` commands passed + all configured artifact validators passed. Nothing more, nothing less.
480	
481	### Optional: `.yoke/last-check.json` manifest
482	
483	Users who want a structured manifest from a Claude Stop hook into the dashboard can emit this shape:
484	
485	```json
486	{
487	  "hook_version": "1",
488	  "ran_at": "...",
489	  "gates": [
490	    { "name": "typecheck", "ok": true, "duration_ms": 1203 },
491	    { "name": "lint",      "ok": true, "duration_ms": 890 },
492	    { "name": "test",      "ok": true, "duration_ms": 5420, "test_count": 42, "pass_count": 42 },
493	    { "name": "build",     "ok": true, "duration_ms": 8100 }
494	  ]
495	}
496	```
497	
498	If present, dashboard renders it as part of the phase summary. If absent, nothing breaks — the user is either gating through `post:` commands instead or has chosen not to gate. No tamper check runs by default; a user wanting one configures a `post:` command that asserts checksums of their hook files.
499	
500	### Example-template quality gating (opt-in)
501	
502	The bundled example template set is designed to work out of the box if the user opts in at `yoke init`:
503	
504	- **PreToolUse safety template** — denies writes outside the worktree path, denies reads of `~/.ssh`, `~/.aws`, `~/.config/gh`, `~/.netrc`, `~/.gnupg`, and curated Bash deny-list patterns. `safety_mode: strict | default | yolo` toggles aggressiveness.
505	- **Stop quality template** — runs project-type-detected typecheck/lint/test/build commands and emits `.yoke/last-check.json`.
506	
507	These are examples. Users may edit, delete, or ignore them entirely. Yoke does not re-install them on subsequent runs.
508	
509	### Hook failure modes (when a user *does* install hooks)
510	
511	Documented in `docs/research/hook-semantics.md` after Phase γ. Yoke's interaction surface with hooks is: it spawns the agent, the agent (or Claude runtime) invokes hooks, the agent session exits. Yoke interprets the exit as it would any other session outcome. Hook *internals* are the user's problem.
512	
513	### Hook exit code semantics (to verify in Phase γ research) [D29]
514	
515	Research task captures real Claude Code hook behavior: exit code meanings, stdin JSON schema, stdout JSON schema, timeout behavior. Documented in `docs/research/hook-semantics.md`. Yoke templates refer to that document rather than hard-coding guesses.
516

518	
519	## Worktree Management [D40]
520	
521	### Lifecycle
522	
523	```
524	User starts workflow "add-auth"
525	  │
526	  ├─► Harness creates worktree at .worktrees/yoke-add-auth-<shortid>
527	  │   (branch: yoke/add-auth-<shortid>, never reused)
528	  │
529	  ├─► Runs bootstrap commands (own phase in state machine)
530	  │    on success → ready
531	  │    on failure → bootstrap_failed (no auto-cleanup; user inspects)
532	  │
533	  ├─► All agent sessions run in the worktree directory
534	  │
535	  ├─► Commits land on yoke/add-auth-<shortid>
536	  │
537	  ├─► On completion: harness can create PR
538	  │
539	  └─► Cleanup: kill tracked child pids → run .yoke/teardown.sh →
540	      git worktree remove --force → branch handling
541	```
542	
543	### Rules
544	
545	- **Branch naming:** always suffix with short workflow id; never reuse.

519	## Worktree Management [D40]
520	
521	### Lifecycle
522	
523	```
524	User starts workflow "add-auth"
525	  │
526	  ├─► Harness creates worktree at .worktrees/yoke-add-auth-<shortid>
527	  │   (branch: yoke/add-auth-<shortid>, never reused)
528	  │
529	  ├─► Runs bootstrap commands (own phase in state machine)
530	  │    on success → ready
531	  │    on failure → bootstrap_failed (no auto-cleanup; user inspects)
532	  │
533	  ├─► All agent sessions run in the worktree directory
534	  │
535	  ├─► Commits land on yoke/add-auth-<shortid>
536	  │
537	  ├─► On completion: harness can create PR
538	  │
539	  └─► Cleanup: kill tracked child pids → run .yoke/teardown.sh →
540	      git worktree remove --force → branch handling
541	```
542	
543	### Rules
544	
545	- **Branch naming:** always suffix with short workflow id; never reuse.
546	- **Bootstrap atomicity:** bootstrap_failed is a terminal state pending user action. No auto-cleanup.
547	- **Gitignored state reproduction:** bootstrap is responsible for reproducing `.env`, containers, etc. Document this clearly in the config guide.
548	- **Teardown:** `.yoke/teardown.sh` runs before `git worktree remove` — stops containers, closes sockets, etc.
549	- **Cleanup order:** kill children → teardown → remove worktree → handle branch.
550	- **Unpushed commits:** never auto-cleanup a worktree whose branch has unpushed commits unless PR creation succeeded.
551	
552	### Configuration
553	
554	```yaml
555	worktrees:
556	  base_dir: ".worktrees"
557	  branch_prefix: "yoke/"
558	  auto_cleanup: true
559	  cleanup_tool: "git"       # git | lazyworktree | custom
560	  bootstrap:
561	    commands:
562	      - "pnpm install"
563	  teardown:
564	    script: ".yoke/teardown.sh"
565	```
566

568	
569	## Process Management
570	
571	### Spawning [D21, D22, D55]
572	
573	Spawn is command-agnostic. Each phase declares `command: string` and `args: string[]` in the pipeline config. Default is `claude`. Users who want profile scoping configure `command: "jig"` with `args: ["run", "<profile>", "--", ...]`.
574	
575	```ts
576	const child = spawn(phase.command, phase.args, {
577	  cwd: worktreePath,
578	  stdio: ['pipe', 'pipe', 'pipe'],
579	  env: { ...process.env, ...phaseEnv, ...correlationEnv },
580	  detached: true,       // own process group for clean shutdown
581	});
582	
583	child.stdin.on('error', handleEpipe);
584	child.stdin.end(promptBuffer);  // one-shot, in-memory
585	
586	const lines = readline.createInterface({ input: child.stdout, crlfDelay: Infinity });
587	lines.on('line', parseStreamJsonLine);
588	
589	child.stderr.on('data', captureStderrWithCap);
590	child.on('error', handleSpawnError);  // ENOENT lands here, not stderr
591	child.on('exit', handleExit);
592	```
593	
594	Prompt size ceiling validated at assembly time (default 4 MB). Larger → phase fails with a clear error.
595	
596	### Shutdown
597	
598	- SIGTERM on the process group: `process.kill(-child.pid, 'SIGTERM')`.
599	- Wait 10 s.
600	- SIGKILL escalation: `process.kill(-child.pid, 'SIGKILL')`.
601	- `sessions` rows carry `{pid, pgid, started_at}` for post-crash reaping.
602	- `yoke doctor` command lists dangling processes.
603	
604	### Heartbeat [D23]
605	
606	Two signals, not one:
607	1. **Liveness** — PID alive AND (if inside a tool_use) the tool hasn't exceeded its per-tool wall-clock budget.
608	2. **Stream activity** — stdout-silence threshold applies **only outside** a tool_use.
609	
610	Heartbeat **never kills**. It emits a `SystemNotice` warning. User decides. Defaults: activity-timeout 90 s outside tool_use; per-tool budgets configurable.
611	
612	---
613	
614	## Crash Recovery [D26, D27]
615	
616	### Core rule
617	
618	`-c` is a soft optimization for intra-phase continuation. **Crash recovery does not depend on `-c`.** Recovery is always a fresh session; the harness reconstructs context from artifacts + `progress.md` + `handoff.json` + git log + Session Log Store.

612	---
613	
614	## Crash Recovery [D26, D27]
615	
616	### Core rule
617	
618	`-c` is a soft optimization for intra-phase continuation. **Crash recovery does not depend on `-c`.** Recovery is always a fresh session; the harness reconstructs context from artifacts + `progress.md` + `handoff.json` + git log + Session Log Store.
619	
620	### On harness SIGTERM/SIGINT
621	
622	1. Every transition is already persisted (SQLite + fsync'd WAL); nothing to save.
623	2. SIGTERM each child process group.
624	3. Wait 10 s; SIGKILL stragglers.
625	4. Exit.
626	
627	### On harness restart
628	
629	1. Load workflow state from SQLite.
630	2. For each `in_progress` workflow:
631	   - Probe each session PID with `kill(pid, 0)`; mark stale sessions `cancelled`.
632	   - Inspect worktree: if `git status` is dirty, auto-stash as `yoke-crash-stash-<ts>` (configurable to `commit-wip`). Surface stash in UI.
633	   - For `review` state: delete any partial `reviews/feature-N/*` files so re-run starts clean.
634	   - Enter `awaiting_user` by default: user clicks "Resume" to fire a fresh session (or "Cancel").
635	   - Optionally auto-resume if `auto_resume: true` is set; default is `false` (laptop-primary target expects user acknowledgment after wake). [D60]
636	
637	### Dashboard surface
638	
639	Workflow cards show a `recoveryState` banner after a restart: `{ recoveredAt, priorStatus, resumeMethod, uncommittedChanges, lastKnownSessionId }`. Persists until user acknowledges.
640	
641	---

643	## SQLite Schema [D38]
644	
645	WAL mode explicit. All state transitions wrapped in transactions. Dashboard reads use a separate read-only connection.
646	
647	```sql
648	PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL;
649	PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL;
650	PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON;
651	
652	CREATE TABLE schema_migrations (
653	  version INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
654	  applied_at TEXT NOT NULL
655	);
656	
657	CREATE TABLE workflows (
658	  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
659	  name TEXT NOT NULL,
660	  spec TEXT NOT NULL,
661	  pipeline TEXT NOT NULL,       -- JSON: phase graph
662	  config TEXT NOT NULL,         -- JSON: resolved config
663	  status TEXT NOT NULL,
664	  worktree_path TEXT,
665	  branch_name TEXT,
666	  recovery_state TEXT,          -- JSON when applicable
667	  created_at TEXT NOT NULL,
668	  updated_at TEXT NOT NULL

643	## SQLite Schema [D38]
644	
645	WAL mode explicit. All state transitions wrapped in transactions. Dashboard reads use a separate read-only connection.
646	
647	```sql
648	PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL;
649	PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL;
650	PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON;
651	
652	CREATE TABLE schema_migrations (
653	  version INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
654	  applied_at TEXT NOT NULL
655	);
656	
657	CREATE TABLE workflows (
658	  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
659	  name TEXT NOT NULL,
660	  spec TEXT NOT NULL,
661	  pipeline TEXT NOT NULL,       -- JSON: phase graph
662	  config TEXT NOT NULL,         -- JSON: resolved config
663	  status TEXT NOT NULL,
664	  worktree_path TEXT,
665	  branch_name TEXT,
666	  recovery_state TEXT,          -- JSON when applicable
667	  created_at TEXT NOT NULL,
668	  updated_at TEXT NOT NULL
669	);
670	CREATE INDEX idx_workflows_status ON workflows(status);
671	
672	CREATE TABLE features (
673	  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
674	  workflow_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workflows(id),
675	  feature_data TEXT NOT NULL,   -- JSON: canonical feature object

677	  current_phase TEXT,           -- free-text label
678	  depends_on TEXT NOT NULL,     -- JSON array of feature ids
679	  retry_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0,

681	  created_by_session_id TEXT,

684	  updated_at TEXT NOT NULL
685	);
686	CREATE INDEX idx_features_workflow ON features(workflow_id);
687	CREATE INDEX idx_features_status ON features(workflow_id, status);
688	
689	CREATE TABLE sessions (
690	  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
691	  workflow_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workflows(id),
692	  feature_id TEXT REFERENCES features(id),
693	  parent_session_id TEXT REFERENCES sessions(id),  -- for -c chains
694	  phase TEXT NOT NULL,
695	  agent_profile TEXT NOT NULL,
696	  pid INTEGER,
697	  pgid INTEGER,
698	  started_at TEXT NOT NULL,
699	  ended_at TEXT,
700	  exit_code INTEGER,
701	  input_tokens INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
702	  output_tokens INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
703	  cache_creation_input_tokens INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
704	  cache_read_input_tokens INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
705	  raw_usage TEXT,
706	  session_log_path TEXT,
707	  status TEXT NOT NULL,
708	  status_flags TEXT,            -- JSON: parse_errors, tainted, manifest_missing, etc.
709	  last_event_at TEXT,
710	  last_event_type TEXT
711	);
712	CREATE INDEX idx_sessions_workflow ON sessions(workflow_id, feature_id);
713	
714	CREATE TABLE events (
715	  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
716	  ts TEXT NOT NULL,
717	  workflow_id TEXT,
718	  feature_id TEXT,
719	  session_id TEXT,
720	  phase TEXT,
721	  attempt INTEGER,
722	  event_type TEXT NOT NULL,
723	  level TEXT NOT NULL,          -- debug|info|warn|error
724	  message TEXT NOT NULL,
725	  extra TEXT                    -- JSON
726	);
727	CREATE INDEX idx_events_workflow ON events(workflow_id, ts);
728	
729	CREATE TABLE artifact_writes (
730	  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
731	  session_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES sessions(id),
732	  artifact_path TEXT NOT NULL,
733	  written_at TEXT NOT NULL,
734	  sha256 TEXT NOT NULL
735	);
736	CREATE INDEX idx_artifact_writes_session ON artifact_writes(session_id);
737	
738	CREATE TABLE pending_attention (
739	  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
740	  workflow_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workflows(id),
741	  kind TEXT NOT NULL,           -- e.g. "blocked_feature", "crash_recovery_required"
742	  payload TEXT NOT NULL,        -- JSON

738	CREATE TABLE pending_attention (
739	  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
740	  workflow_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES workflows(id),
741	  kind TEXT NOT NULL,           -- e.g. "blocked_feature", "crash_recovery_required"
742	  payload TEXT NOT NULL,        -- JSON
743	  created_at TEXT NOT NULL,
744	  acknowledged_at TEXT
