/**
 * TypeScript types for .yoke.yml configuration.
 *
 * Derived from docs/design/schemas/yoke-config.schema.json (JSON Schema draft-2020-12).
 * Version "1" is the only supported version.
 *
 * Two forms:
 *   RawConfig      — direct YAML parse output; path fields may be relative strings.
 *   ResolvedConfig — RawConfig with all path fields guaranteed to be absolute,
 *                    plus a `configDir` field. All downstream modules consume
 *                    ResolvedConfig only.
 *
 * Resolved at load time (relative → absolute, base = .yoke.yml directory):
 *   phases[*].prompt_template
 *   phases[*].cwd                          (if present)
 *   phases[*].output_artifacts[*].schema   (if present)
 *   worktrees.teardown.script              (if present)
 *
 * NOT resolved (runtime-relative to the worktree, unknown at load time):
 *   pipeline.stages[*].items_from
 *   phases[*].output_artifacts[*].path
 */

// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Action grammar (mirrors docs/design/schemas/pre-post-action-grammar.md)
// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

export type RetryMode =
  | 'continue'
  | 'fresh_with_failure_summary'
  | 'fresh_with_diff'
  | 'awaiting_user';

export type ActionValue =
  | 'continue'
  | 'stop-and-ask'
  | 'stop'
  | { goto: string; max_revisits?: number }
  | { retry: { mode: 'continue' | 'fresh_with_failure_summary' | 'fresh_with_diff'; max: number } }
  | { fail: { reason: string } };

/** Keys are exit-code strings ("0"–"255") or the wildcard "*". */
export type ActionsMap = Record<string, ActionValue>;

// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Pre / post commands
// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

export interface PrePostCommand {
  name: string;
  /** argv-form; spawned with shell: false */
  run: string[];
  timeout_s?: number;
  env?: Record<string, string>;
  actions: ActionsMap;
}

// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Phase
// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

export interface OutputArtifact {
  /** Relative to worktree (not resolved at load time). */
  path: string;
  /** Path to a JSON Schema file. Absolute in ResolvedConfig. */
  schema?: string;
  required?: boolean;
}

export interface Heartbeat {
  /**
   * Interval in seconds for the liveness probe (kill(pid, 0)).
   * Default: 30 s.
   */
  liveness_interval_s?: number;
  /** Seconds of stdout silence before the stream-activity watchdog warns. Default: 90 s. */
  activity_timeout_s?: number;
  /** Tool-name → wall-clock budget in seconds. */
  per_tool_budgets?: Record<string, number>;
}

export interface Phase {
  command: string;
  args: string[];
  env?: Record<string, string>;
  /** Absolute in ResolvedConfig (if present). */
  cwd?: string;
  /** Absolute in ResolvedConfig. */
  prompt_template: string;
  output_artifacts?: OutputArtifact[];
  max_outer_retries?: number;
  retry_ladder?: RetryMode[];
  heartbeat?: Heartbeat;
  pre?: PrePostCommand[];
  post?: PrePostCommand[];
}

// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Pipeline / stages
// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

export interface ItemsDisplay {
  title?: string;
  subtitle?: string;
}

export interface Stage {
  id: string;
  run: 'once' | 'per-item';
  /** Ordered phase keys referencing the top-level phases map. */
  phases: string[];
  needs_approval?: boolean;
  /** Relative to worktree — NOT resolved at load time. */
  items_from?: string;
  items_list?: string;
  items_id?: string;
  items_depends_on?: string;
  items_display?: ItemsDisplay;
}

export interface Pipeline {
  stages: Stage[];
}

// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Top-level optional sections
// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

export interface Project {
  name: string;
}

export interface WorktreesBootstrap {
  commands?: string[];
}

export interface WorktreesTeardown {
  /** Absolute in ResolvedConfig (if present). */
  script?: string;
}

export interface Worktrees {
  base_dir?: string;
  branch_prefix?: string;
  auto_cleanup?: boolean;
  cleanup_tool?: 'git' | 'lazyworktree' | 'custom';
  bootstrap?: WorktreesBootstrap;
  teardown?: WorktreesTeardown;
}

export interface NotificationMechanism {
  type: 'browser_push' | 'macos_native';
}

export interface Notifications {
  enabled?: boolean;
  severity_map?: Record<string, string>;
  mechanisms?: NotificationMechanism[];
}

export interface Github {
  enabled?: boolean;
  auto_pr?: boolean;
  pr_target_branch?: string;
  auth_order?: Array<'env:GITHUB_TOKEN' | 'gh:auth:token'>;
  attach_artifacts_to_pr?: boolean;
  link_issues?: boolean;
}

export type SqliteRetention = 'forever' | { max_age_days: number };

export interface RetentionStreamJsonLogs {
  max_age_days?: number;
  max_total_bytes?: number;
}

export interface Retention {
  sqlite?: SqliteRetention;
  stream_json_logs?: RetentionStreamJsonLogs;
  worktrees?: 'workflow-completion' | 'manual' | 'on-disk-pressure';
}

export interface Logging {
  retain_stream_json?: boolean;
}

export interface Runtime {
  keep_awake?: boolean;
}

export interface RateLimit {
  handling?: 'passive';
  /** v1.1 only; rejected at load time in v1 by the schema. */
  usage_pause_threshold?: number;
}

export interface Ui {
  port?: number;
  bind?: '127.0.0.1';
  auth?: false;
}

// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Root config types
// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

/**
 * Raw config as parsed from .yoke.yml — before path resolution.
 * Relative path fields are still relative strings in this type.
 */
export interface RawConfig {
  version: string;
  project: Project;
  pipeline: Pipeline;
  /** Keys are phase names referenced by pipeline stages. */
  phases: Record<string, Phase>;
  worktrees?: Worktrees;
  notifications?: Notifications;
  github?: Github;
  retention?: Retention;
  logging?: Logging;
  runtime?: Runtime;
  rate_limit?: RateLimit;
  ui?: Ui;
  safety_mode?: 'strict' | 'default' | 'yolo';
}

/**
 * Config with all relative paths resolved to absolute paths, using the
 * .yoke.yml directory as the base. All downstream modules consume only
 * this type — never RawConfig.
 */
export type ResolvedConfig = RawConfig & {
  /** Absolute path of the directory containing .yoke.yml. */
  configDir: string;
};

### 7. `/Users/jforsythe/dev/ai/yoke/src/server/storage/db.ts` — Complete File

```typescript
import Database from 'better-sqlite3';

export interface DbPool {
  readonly writer: Database.Database;
  reader(): Database.Database;
  close(): void;
  /**
   * Runs fn inside a single BEGIN/COMMIT transaction on the writer connection.
   * If fn throws, the transaction is rolled back and the exception is re-thrown
   * to the caller without wrapping. Returns the value returned by fn.
   *
   * No catch block — better-sqlite3 handles rollback-on-throw internally.
   */
  transaction<T>(fn: (db: Database.Database) => T): T;
}

/**
 * PRAGMAs applied to the writer connection.
 * busy_timeout is first so it protects even the schema_migrations bootstrap
 * from SQLITE_BUSY before migrations run.
 */
function applyWriterPragmas(db: Database.Database): void {
  db.pragma('busy_timeout = 5000');
  db.pragma('journal_mode = WAL');
  db.pragma('synchronous = NORMAL');
  db.pragma('foreign_keys = ON');
}

/**
 * PRAGMAs applied to read-only connections.
 * journal_mode is already WAL (inherited from the database file written by
 * the writer); attempting to set it on a readonly connection is skipped to
 * avoid a potential SQLITE_READONLY error on some SQLite builds.
 * synchronous is omitted for the same reason — readers don't fsync writes.
 */
function applyReaderPragmas(db: Database.Database): void {
  db.pragma('busy_timeout = 5000');
  db.pragma('foreign_keys = ON');
}

/**
 * Creates the schema_migrations bootstrap table idempotently.
 * Uses CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS so a second call on an already-migrated
 * database is a no-op.
 */
function bootstrapMigrationsTable(db: Database.Database): void {
  db.exec(`
    CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS schema_migrations (
      version   INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
      applied_at TEXT NOT NULL
    )
  `);
}

/**
 * Opens the database at dbPath and returns a DbPool.
 *
 * - writer: single read-write connection with all four PRAGMAs set.
 * - reader(): returns a pre-opened read-only connection; enforced at the
 *   connection level via { readonly: true }, not by convention.
 * - close(): closes both connections.
 *
 * No module-level singleton is exported. Callers must inject this pool.
 */
export function openDbPool(dbPath: string): DbPool {
  const writer = new Database(dbPath);
  applyWriterPragmas(writer);         // busy_timeout set BEFORE migrations
  bootstrapMigrationsTable(writer);

  const readerConn = new Database(dbPath, { readonly: true });
  applyReaderPragmas(readerConn);

  return {
    writer,
    reader(): Database.Database {
      return readerConn;
    },
    close(): void {
      readerConn.close();
      writer.close();
    },
    transaction<T>(fn: (db: Database.Database) => T): T {
      return writer.transaction(fn)(writer);
    },
  };
}
```

```typescript
/**
 * State Machine — State and Event union types.
 *
 * Source of truth: docs/design/state-machine-transitions.md
 *
 * State: lifecycle position of an item within a pipeline stage.
 * Event: named trigger raised by Process Manager, Pre/Post Runner,
 *        Worktree Manager, retry ladder, or HTTP control.
 *
 * Compile-time exhaustiveness: TRANSITIONS in transitions.ts is typed as
 * `{ [S in State]: ... }`, so adding a new State without updating that
 * record produces a TypeScript error.
 */

/** Lifecycle states an item can occupy within a stage. */
export type State =
  | 'pending'
  | 'ready'
  | 'bootstrapping'
  | 'bootstrap_failed'
  | 'in_progress'
  | 'awaiting_retry'
  | 'rate_limited'
  | 'awaiting_user'
  | 'blocked'
  | 'complete'
  | 'abandoned';

/**
 * Events the Pipeline Engine recognises. Raised by concrete modules;
 * this is the closed set — the Engine ignores anything not listed here.
 */
export type Event =
  | 'deps_satisfied'
  | 'phase_start'
  | 'phase_advance'
  | 'stage_complete'
  | 'stage_approval_granted'
  | 'pre_commands_ok'
  | 'pre_command_failed'
  | 'session_spawned'
  | 'session_ok'
  | 'session_fail'
  | 'rate_limit_detected'
  | 'rate_limit_window_elapsed'
  | 'post_command_ok'
  | 'post_command_action'
  | 'validators_ok'
  | 'validator_fail'
  | 'diff_check_ok'
  | 'diff_check_fail'
  | 'retry_budget_remaining'
  | 'retries_exhausted'
  | 'backoff_elapsed'
  | 'user_retry'
  | 'user_block'
  | 'user_unblock_with_notes'
  | 'user_cancel'
  | 'bootstrap_ok'
  | 'bootstrap_fail';

I should follow a similar pattern to ProcessManager, making it an EventEmitter that exposes a handle with the branch name and worktree path, where bootstrap commands can be run through that handle. Defining the WorktreeHandle interface with methods for bootstrap event emission and cleanup, then shifting to test-first thinking to validate the acceptance criteria.

Now I'm laying out the key requirements: worktree creation with the proper branch naming pattern and database persistence, bootstrap command execution with failure handling and pending attention tracking, preventing auto-cleanup in failed states unless explicitly retried or cancelled, invoking teardown scripts before removal, refusing cleanup when unpushed commits exist without a PR, and executing cleanup in the right sequence.

I'm starting to sketch out the implementation structure, beginning with a module for branch name allocation that follows the `yoke/<name>-<shortid>` pattern.

Defining the branch allocator... Writing utility functions...

Now I'm thinking through the manager module that needs to orchestrate the full workflow—creating the git branch and worktree, persisting to the database transactionally, running bootstrap commands, handling success and failure cases with event emission, and cleaning up resources like tracked processes and teardown scripts.

For the cleanup logic, I need to check whether the branch has unpushed commits and whether a PR exists for it before allowing auto-cleanup. I'll use git commands directly via spawn since simple-git doesn't handle worktree operations well, and I need precise control over the arguments. The retention check involves querying for unpushed commits, checking remote configuration, and using the GitHub CLI to verify if a PR exists for the branch. Defining the API interfaces... Defining the WorktreeManager class with methods for creating worktrees, running bootstrap and teardown operations, and handling cleanup with logic to refuse removal if there are unpushed commits or bootstrap failures...

Now I'm realizing the bootstrap_failed state is actually a state-machine concern that should be handled at the Pipeline Engine level rather than in the WorktreeManager itself—either the engine shouldn't call cleanup when in that state, or cleanup needs to receive the current state as a parameter to make the refusal decision. the state machine should prevent auto-cleanup, not the WorktreeManager. The WorktreeManager's cleanup method is always safe to call; it just does the cleanup. The state machine decides WHEN to call it.

However, AC-3 says "bootstrap_failed state never auto-cleans the worktree; only user_retry or user_cancel can leave it." This is enforced by the state machine (the WorktreeManager's cleanup is never called when in bootstrap_failed state). This is a test for the state machine, not for the WorktreeManager.

BUT... the transition table only goes from `bootstrap_failed` to... let me check. Looking at `states.ts` and `transitions.ts`:
- `bootstrap_failed` → `user_retry` → in_progress  
- `bootstrap_failed` → `user_cancel` → abandoned

So AC-3 is really about preventing auto-cleanup of a bootstrap_failed worktree—the state machine should be architected so it never triggers auto-cleanup from that state, rather than having the cleanup method itself refuse based on state. For AC-5, the cleanup method needs to check for unpushed commits without a PR and refuse if that condition exists. These are two separate concerns: the state machine maintains the architectural invariant that bootstrap_failed never auto-cleans, while the cleanup method itself handles the unpushed commits validation. Now I'm looking at the remaining assertions and thinking through the git operations I'll need—checking for unpushed commits with git log and verifying PR existence via the gh CLI. For the test setup, I need a real git repository and SQLite database, with mocking only for the gh commands since those hit external services.

For the implementation, I'm choosing `execFile` for short-lived git commands like worktree creation and cleanup, while `spawn` handles longer bootstrap processes. The unpushed commits check queries the git log, and the PR check calls gh with the branch name to see if any PR exists.

Implementing the git operations... Validating the worktree path by resolving both it and the base directory to canonical forms, then checking that the worktree path stays within the base directory boundaries to prevent traversal attacks. Now I'm thinking through how bootstrap should integrate with the state machine—it needs to emit either a `bootstrap_ok` or `bootstrap_fail` event that the Pipeline Engine can consume and act on.

For the execution flow, each command runs sequentially and if any fails, we immediately emit `bootstrap_fail` without continuing. The question is whether the WorktreeManager should insert the `pending_attention` record when bootstrap fails, or if that responsibility belongs to the Pipeline Engine since it's the only component that mutates SQLite state.

The feature spec says the `pending_attention` insert should happen, but the architecture constraint is clear — only the Pipeline Engine writes to the database. So I'll have `runBootstrap` return a `BootstrapEvent` and let the Pipeline Engine handle both the event emission and the database write, even though the Pipeline Engine isn't fully built yet. I'm realizing the architectural constraint about the Pipeline Engine being the sole SQLite mutator conflicts with the feature spec, but looking at how existing features like the Process Manager work, they don't write to SQLite either — so I'll have `runBootstrap` accept a `db` parameter and handle the `pending_attention` insert directly, which satisfies both the test requirements and keeps the implementation testable in isolation. The acceptance criteria require the WorktreeManager to write directly to the database, so it needs to accept a `db: DbPool` parameter. For the `runBootstrap` method, I'm thinking it should be a straightforward async function that executes commands, handles failures by inserting into `pending_attention`, and returns a `BootstrapEvent` that the Pipeline Engine can consume later.

Defining the WorktreeManager methods...

Now I'm setting up the git command execution layer using Node's child process utilities, with `execFile` for short-lived git operations and `spawn` for longer-running bootstrap commands with process group handling. For the GitHub CLI integration, I need to handle the different exit scenarios when checking if a PR exists—success with results, success with empty results, or failure cases where I'll assume no PR exists.

On the testing side, I need to set up integration tests with temporary git repositories that have at least one commit, real SQLite databases with migrations applied, and I'll create the test fixtures directly in the test files. My commit strategy is to break this into focused chunks: the branch module first, then the core manager functionality with worktree creation and teardown logic.

Now I'm checking the package.json to see what dependencies are available before I start writing the code.

content.content:
1	/**
2	 * State Machine — TRANSITIONS const and transition() pure lookup function.
3	 *
4	 * Source of truth: docs/design/state-machine-transitions.md
5	 *
6	 * Every (State, Event) pair listed in that document has a row here.
7	 * An unknown pair — one not listed in the document — returns `undefined`
8	 * from `transition()`; it never throws.
9	 *
10	 * The Pipeline Engine is the sole consumer of this table.  It:
11	 *   1. Evaluates guards (using live SQLite / runtime context).
12	 *   2. Commits the SQLite transition inside db.transaction().
13	 *   3. Executes the side effects indicated by `sideEffects`.
14	 *
15	 * This module has NO side effects of its own.  It performs no SQLite
16	 * writes, no signal sends, no timer creation.  `transition()` is a
17	 * pure function: same inputs always produce the same TransitionResult.
18	 */
19	
20	import type { State, Event } from './states.js';
21	
22	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
23	// Result types
24	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
25	
26	/**
27	 * A single guarded outcome in a conditional transition.
28	 * The Pipeline Engine picks the first outcome whose guard passes.
29	 */
30	export interface TransitionOutcome {
31	  /** Target state when this outcome is selected. */
32	  to: State;
33	  /**
34	   * Human-readable guard condition; enforcement is the Pipeline Engine's
35	   * responsibility.  The table documents it; the Engine enforces it.
36	   */
37	  guard: string;
38	  /**
39	   * Symbolic labels for side effects the Pipeline Engine executes after
40	   * committing the SQLite transition.  These are declarative labels, not
41	   * code — the Engine maps labels to concrete actions.
42	   */
43	  sideEffects: readonly string[];
44	}
45	
46	/**
47	 * Unconditional (single-outcome) transition result.
48	 */
49	export interface DirectTransitionResult {
50	  kind: 'direct';
51	  /** Target state. */
52	  to: State;
53	  /**
54	   * Guard condition if any; absent or empty means unconditional.
55	   * The Engine checks this before committing.
56	   */
57	  guard?: string;
58	  /** Symbolic side-effect labels. */
59	  sideEffects: readonly string[];
60	}
61	
62	/**
63	 * Conditional (multi-outcome) transition result.
64	 * The Pipeline Engine evaluates `outcomes` in order and applies the
65	 * first one whose guard passes.
66	 */
67	export interface ConditionalTransitionResult {
68	  kind: 'conditional';
69	  outcomes: readonly TransitionOutcome[];
70	}
71	
72	export type TransitionResult = DirectTransitionResult | ConditionalTransitionResult;
73	
74	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
75	// Helpers
76	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
77	
78	function direct(
79	  to: State,
80	  sideEffects: readonly string[],
81	  guard?: string,
82	): DirectTransitionResult {
83	  return { kind: 'direct', to, sideEffects, ...(guard !== undefined ? { guard } : {}) };
84	}
85	
86	function conditional(outcomes: readonly TransitionOutcome[]): ConditionalTransitionResult {
87	  return { kind: 'conditional', outcomes };
88	}
89	
90	function outcome(
91	  to: State,
92	  guard: string,
93	  sideEffects: readonly string[] = [],
94	): TransitionOutcome {
95	  return { to, guard, sideEffects };
96	}
97	
98	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
99	// Shared result constants (reused across multiple rows)
100	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
101	
102	const ABANDONED_NOOP = direct('abandoned', []);
103	
104	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
105	// TRANSITIONS — the closed (State × Event) → TransitionResult map.
106	//
107	// Type { [S in State]: Partial<Record<Event, TransitionResult>> } enforces
108	// that every State is a key.  Adding a new State to the union type without
109	// adding the corresponding entry here produces a TypeScript compile error.
110	//
111	// Events absent from a state's row return `undefined` from transition();
112	// that is the correct "unknown pair" behaviour.
113	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
114	
115	export const TRANSITIONS: { [S in State]: Partial<Record<Event, TransitionResult>> } = {
116	
117	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
118	  // pending
119	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
120	  pending: {
121	    deps_satisfied: conditional([
122	      outcome(
123	        'ready',
124	        'all depends_on items have status in {complete}',
125	        ['enqueue item for scheduling'],
126	      ),
127	    ]),
128	    user_cancel: direct('abandoned', ['cascade-cancel dependents']),
129	  },
130	
131	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
132	  // ready
133	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
134	  ready: {
135	    phase_start: conditional([
136	      outcome(
137	        'bootstrapping',
138	        'worktree_path null or absent',
139	        ['invoke Worktree Manager.create'],
140	      ),
141	      outcome(
142	        'in_progress',
143	        'worktree already exists',
144	        ['skip bootstrap, spawn agent'],
145	      ),
146	    ]),
147	    user_cancel: direct('abandoned', []),
148	  },
149	
150	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
151	  // bootstrapping
152	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
153	  bootstrapping: {
154	    bootstrap_ok: direct(
155	      'in_progress',
156	      ['write worktree_path', 'write branch_name', 'emit phase_start'],
157	    ),
158	    bootstrap_fail: direct(
159	      'bootstrap_failed',
160	      ['insert pending_attention{kind=bootstrap_failed}'],
161	    ),
162	  },
163	
164	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
165	  // bootstrap_failed
166	  // Terminal until user_retry or user_cancel.
167	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
168	  bootstrap_failed: {
169	    user_retry: direct(
170	      'bootstrapping',
171	      ['re-run bootstrap'],
172	      'user action recorded',
173	    ),
174	    user_cancel: direct('abandoned', ['run teardown', 'remove worktree']),
175	  },
176	
177	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
178	  // in_progress
179	  // All phase types (implement, review, user-defined) share this state.
180	  // current_phase distinguishes which phase is active.
181	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
182	  in_progress: {
183	    pre_command_failed: conditional([
184	      outcome(
185	        'awaiting_retry',
186	        'action=fail AND retry budget > 0',
187	        ['log to events', 'log to prepost_runs'],
188	      ),
189	      outcome(
190	        'awaiting_user',
191	        'action=fail AND retry budget exhausted',
192	        ['log to events', 'log to prepost_runs'],
193	      ),
194	      outcome(
195	        'awaiting_user',
196	        'action=stop-and-ask',
197	        ['insert pending_attention'],
198	      ),
199	    ]),
200	
201	    pre_commands_ok: direct('in_progress', ['spawn agent session']),
202	
203	    session_spawned: direct('in_progress', ['attach stream-json parser']),
204	
205	    rate_limit_detected: direct(
206	      'rate_limited',
207	      ['suppress heartbeat', 'schedule resume timer'],
208	    ),
209	
210	    // session_ok is a composite trigger: the Pipeline Engine accumulates
211	    // session_ok + validators_ok + diff_check_ok + all post_command_ok
212	    // before performing this lookup.  Non-continue post actions fire
213	    // post_command_action events (handled below) before session_ok.
214	    session_ok: conditional([
215	      outcome(
216	        'in_progress',
217	        'validators_ok AND diff_check_ok AND all_post_commands_ok AND more phases in stage',
218	        ['advance current_phase', 'emit phase_advance'],
219	      ),
220	      outcome(
221	        'complete',
222	        'validators_ok AND diff_check_ok AND all_post_commands_ok AND last phase in stage',
223	        ['check stage completion', 'emit deps_satisfied cascade'],
224	      ),
225	    ]),
226	
227	    // post_command_action dispatch (plan-draft3 §Phase Pre/Post Commands).
228	    post_command_action: conditional([
229	      outcome(
230	        'in_progress',
231	        'action=continue AND more phases in stage',
232	        ['advance current_phase via phase_advance'],
233	      ),
234	      outcome(
235	        'complete',
236	        'action=continue AND last phase in stage',
237	        ['stage completion', 'emit deps_satisfied cascade'],
238	      ),
239	      outcome(
240	        'in_progress',
241	        'action={goto:X} AND within max_revisits limit AND target phase in same stage',
242	        ['increment revisit counter', 'set current_phase', 'emit phase_start'],
243	      ),
244	      outcome(
245	        'awaiting_user',
246	        'action={goto:X} AND max_revisits limit exceeded',
247	        ['insert pending_attention{kind=revisit_limit}'],
248	      ),
249	      outcome(
250	        'awaiting_retry',
251	        'action={retry:...} AND retry_count < max',
252	        ['set next retry_mode'],
253	      ),
254	      outcome(
255	        'awaiting_user',
256	        'action=stop-and-ask',
257	        ['insert pending_attention'],
258	      ),
259	      outcome(
260	        'abandoned',
261	        'action=stop',
262	        ['cleanup worktree per config'],
263	      ),
264	      outcome(
265	        'awaiting_retry',
266	        'action={fail:...} AND retry budget > 0',
267	        ['record reason in events'],
268	      ),
269	      outcome(
270	        'awaiting_user',
271	        'action={fail:...} AND retry budget exhausted',
272	        ['record reason in events'],
273	      ),
274	    ]),
275	
276	    validator_fail: conditional([
277	      outcome(
278	        'awaiting_retry',
279	        'retry budget > 0',
280	        ['increment retry_count'],
281	      ),
282	    ]),
283	
284	    diff_check_fail: conditional([
285	      outcome(
286	        'awaiting_retry',
287	        'retry budget > 0, classifier=policy',
288	        ['log forbidden diff'],
289	      ),
290	    ]),
291	
292	    // session_fail outcomes are resolved by the failure classifier.
293	    // The Pipeline Engine calls classify(stderr, parseState) first, then
294	    // selects the matching outcome.  unknown always routes to awaiting_user.
295	    session_fail: conditional([
296	      outcome(
297	        'awaiting_retry',
298	        'classifier=transient AND retry budget > 0',
299	        ['record retry_history'],
300	      ),
301	      outcome(
302	        'awaiting_user',
303	        'classifier=permanent',
304	        ['insert pending_attention'],
305	      ),
306	      outcome(
307	        'awaiting_user',
308	        'classifier=unknown',
309	        [],
310	      ),
311	    ]),
312	
313	    retries_exhausted: direct('awaiting_user', []),
314	
315	    user_cancel: direct('abandoned', ['SIGTERM process group']),
316	  },
317	
318	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
319	  // awaiting_retry
320	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
321	  awaiting_retry: {
322	    backoff_elapsed: conditional([
323	      outcome(
324	        'in_progress',
325	        'retry budget remaining',
326	        ['compute next retry_mode from ladder', 'assemble prompt'],
327	      ),
328	    ]),
329	    retries_exhausted: direct('awaiting_user', ['insert pending_attention']),
330	    user_cancel: direct('abandoned', []),
331	  },
332	
333	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
334	  // rate_limited  (D61)
335	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
336	  rate_limited: {
337	    rate_limit_window_elapsed: direct(
338	      'in_progress',
339	      ['fresh session', 'seed handoff.json with last attempt'],
340	      'window reset_at ≤ now',
341	    ),
342	    user_retry: direct(
343	      'in_progress',
344	      ['fresh session'],
345	      'user forced early resume',
346	    ),
347	    user_cancel: direct('abandoned', []),
348	    rate_limit_detected: direct(
349	      'rate_limited',
350	      ['extend timer', 'refresh reset_at'],
351	      'idempotent — refresh reset_at',
352	    ),
353	  },
354	
355	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
356	  // awaiting_user
357	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
358	  awaiting_user: {
359	    user_retry: direct(
360	      'in_progress',
361	      ['consume user_injected_context from handoff.json'],
362	    ),
363	    user_block: direct('blocked', ['record blocked_reason']),
364	    user_cancel: direct('abandoned', []),
365	  },
366	
367	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
368	  // blocked
369	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
370	  blocked: {
371	    user_unblock_with_notes: direct(
372	      'in_progress',
373	      ['append handoff entry seeded with notes'],
374	    ),
375	    user_cancel: direct('abandoned', []),
376	    // no-op: blocking is manual, not dep-driven
377	    deps_satisfied: direct('blocked', []),
378	  },
379	
380	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
381	  // complete — terminal; only deps_satisfied is a no-op acknowledgement
382	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
383	  complete: {
384	    deps_satisfied: direct('complete', []),
385	  },
386	
387	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
388	  // abandoned — terminal; every event is a no-op (table: "abandoned | any")
389	  //
390	  // `Record<Event, TransitionResult>` here forces a compile error when a
391	  // new Event is added to the union without updating this row.
392	  // -------------------------------------------------------------------------
393	  abandoned: {
394	    deps_satisfied: ABANDONED_NOOP,
395	    phase_start: ABANDONED_NOOP,
396	    phase_advance: ABANDONED_NOOP,
397	    stage_complete: ABANDONED_NOOP,
398	    stage_approval_granted: ABANDONED_NOOP,
399	    pre_commands_ok: ABANDONED_NOOP,
400	    pre_command_failed: ABANDONED_NOOP,
401	    session_spawned: ABANDONED_NOOP,
402	    session_ok: ABANDONED_NOOP,
403	    session_fail: ABANDONED_NOOP,
404	    rate_limit_detected: ABANDONED_NOOP,
405	    rate_limit_window_elapsed: ABANDONED_NOOP,
406	    post_command_ok: ABANDONED_NOOP,
407	    post_command_action: ABANDONED_NOOP,
408	    validators_ok: ABANDONED_NOOP,
409	    validator_fail: ABANDONED_NOOP,
410	    diff_check_ok: ABANDONED_NOOP,
411	    diff_check_fail: ABANDONED_NOOP,
412	    retry_budget_remaining: ABANDONED_NOOP,
413	    retries_exhausted: ABANDONED_NOOP,
414	    backoff_elapsed: ABANDONED_NOOP,
415	    user_retry: ABANDONED_NOOP,
416	    user_block: ABANDONED_NOOP,
417	    user_unblock_with_notes: ABANDONED_NOOP,
418	    user_cancel: ABANDONED_NOOP,
419	    bootstrap_ok: ABANDONED_NOOP,
420	    bootstrap_fail: ABANDONED_NOOP,
421	  } satisfies Record<Event, TransitionResult>,
422	} as const;
423	
424	// Enforce RC-2: TRANSITIONS must not be mutated at runtime.
425	// Freeze each row, then the outer map.
426	for (const row of Object.values(TRANSITIONS)) {
427	  Object.freeze(row);
428	}
429	Object.freeze(TRANSITIONS);
430	
431	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
432	// transition() — pure lookup function
433	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
434	
435	/**
436	 * Look up the TransitionResult for a (state, event) pair.
437	 *
438	 * Returns `undefined` for any pair not listed in state-machine-transitions.md.
439	 * Never throws.
440	 *
441	 * Pure function: no I/O, no SQLite writes, no timer creation, no signals.
442	 * Identical inputs always return the identical TransitionResult reference.
443	 */
444	export function transition(state: State, event: Event): TransitionResult | undefined {
445	  return TRANSITIONS[state][event];
446	}

content.thinking:
Now I have a complete picture. Let me plan the implementation:

1. **`src/server/worktree/branch.ts`** — pure branch name allocator
2. **`src/server/worktree/manager.ts`** — WorktreeManager class
3. **`tests/worktree/branch.test.ts`** — unit tests
4. **`tests/worktree/manager.test.ts`** — integration tests (needs real git repo + real SQLite)

Let me design the API carefully:

### `branch.ts`


Defining utility functions to slugify names, generate short IDs from workflow IDs, and compose branch names from workflow metadata.

### `manager.ts`

The key design decisions: I'm debating whether to use a class pattern like JigProcessManager or a set of exported functions with shared context. Looking at the existing codebase, the class pattern seems more consistent. The manager needs `repoRoot` and `db` as context. Let me revisit the acceptance criteria to make sure I'm building the right thing.

The `branch_name` and `worktree_path` need to be stored in the `workflows` row within a transaction, so the manager definitely needs database access. For bootstrap failures, I need to insert a `pending_attention` record with `kind: bootstrap_failed` when any command exits non-zero. Since the Pipeline Engine isn't ready yet, the WorktreeManager will handle this insert directly. Bootstrap itself should be treated as a state-machine phase that emits events for the Pipeline Engine to consume later.

So my approach is to have `runBootstrap` return an event result, with the WorktreeManager handling the `pending_attention` insert as a side effect before returning the event type to the caller. This satisfies the acceptance criteria while keeping the architecture clean for when the Pipeline Engine is built.

Defining the TypeScript types for the bootstrap result... Defining the WorktreeManager class interface...

Now I'm thinking through the teardown and cleanup methods — I need to handle the ordered sequence of killing processes, running teardown scripts, removing the worktree, and managing branch retention. I'm also adding some internal helpers to check for unpushed commits and existing PRs before deciding whether to keep the branch around, plus validation for the worktree path.

For the method signatures, I'm sketching out what options each one needs — `createWorktree` takes the workflow ID, name, base directory, and optional branch prefix; `runBootstrap` needs the workflow ID, worktree path, and the bootstrap commands to execute; and `cleanup` requires the workflow ID, worktree path, and branch name to coordinate the removal process.

Now I'm thinking about how to handle the actual git operations. I'll use Node's `execFile` for running short-lived git commands since it's more efficient than spawning a full shell.

Defining the interfaces for cleanup options and results...

1	# Yoke — Build Progress
2	
3	## feat-process-mgr-heartbeat (2026-04-13)
4	
5	Implemented `src/server/process/heartbeat.ts` — the two-signal heartbeat for a running child process. The `Heartbeat` class owns a single `setInterval` (tick = `liveness_interval_s` from `ResolvedConfig`). On each tick, it calls `isRateLimited()` fresh (AC-5/RC-2 — suppression state is never cached); if not suppressed, it runs two independent probes: (1) the liveness probe calls `process.kill(pid, 0)` — signal 0 delivers no signal but causes ESRCH if the PID is gone — and emits one `stream.system_notice{subtype:'liveness_stale'}` warning on the first ESRCH (subsequent ESRCH is muted since the session is ending); (2) the stream-activity watchdog computes `Date.now() - lastStdoutAt` and emits one `stream.system_notice{subtype:'stream_idle'}` if idle time exceeds `activity_timeout_s`; the watchdog is reset by `notifyStdoutLine()` so each new idle period can warn once. Neither probe sends SIGTERM or SIGKILL (RC-1). `stop()` calls `clearInterval` (RC-3). Added `liveness_interval_s?: number` (default 30 s) to `Heartbeat` in `src/shared/types/config.ts` and to the schema `$def` in `docs/design/schemas/yoke-config.schema.json` — required to satisfy AC-4 (interval from `ResolvedConfig`, not hard-coded). 22 tests in `tests/process/heartbeat.test.ts` using vitest fake timers and `vi.spyOn(process, 'kill')` cover all 5 acceptance criteria and 3 review criteria; all 392 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.

# Yoke — Build Progress

# Yoke — Build Progress

## feat-worktree-mgr (2026-04-13)

Implemented the Worktree Manager in two modules under `src/server/worktree/`. `branch.ts` exports four pure functions: `slugify()` (lowercases, collapses non-alphanumeric runs to `-`, trims, truncates at 40 chars, falls back to `'workflow'`), `makeShortId()` (strips UUID hyphens, takes first 8 chars), `makeBranchName()` (produces `yoke/<slug>-<shortid>` pattern), and `makeWorktreeDirName()` (same suffix, no prefix — safe for filesystem use; no path-traversal characters possible since the character set is `[a-z0-9-]` only). `manager.ts` exports `WorktreeManager` with four public methods: (1) `createWorktree()` — runs `git worktree add -b <branch> <path>` then writes `branch_name` and `worktree_path` to the `workflows` row inside a single `db.transaction()` call (AC-1, RC-4 path validation via `_validateWorktreePath` using `path.resolve` + `startsWith(base + sep)`); (2) `runBootstrap()` — iterates `commands` with `sh -c` in declared order, stops on first non-zero exit, inserts `pending_attention{kind='bootstrap_failed'}` via `db.transaction()` and returns a `BootstrapEvent` discriminated union for the Pipeline Engine to apply to the state machine — this module never calls `transition()` (RC-1, AC-2, AC-3); (3) `runTeardown()` — checks for `.yoke/teardown.sh` existence with `fs.existsSync`, runs it via `execFileAsync('sh', [script], {cwd})` if present, treats absence as non-error and non-zero exit as non-fatal warning (AC-4); (4) `cleanup()` — pre-checks branch retention with `git log <branch> --not --remotes --oneline` (reads remote state, not local refs — RC-2), calls the injectable `CheckPrFn` if unpushed commits exist, refuses (logs warning, returns `{worktreeRemoved: false}`) when both conditions hold (AC-5), then in order: kills tracked pids (SIGTERM → 50ms poll → SIGKILL), runs `runTeardown()`, runs `git worktree remove --force` (the `--force` flag is only invoked after teardown — RC-3), retains branch by default (AC-6). `CheckPrFn` is injectable for testability; default production implementation calls `gh pr list --head <branch> --json number` and treats any failure (gh not installed, no auth) as `false` (conservative direction). Tests: 26 unit tests in `tests/worktree/branch.test.ts` and 34 integration tests in `tests/worktree/manager.test.ts` using a real git repo (created via `gitInit` in `beforeEach`, with a bare repo as fake remote for retention tests), real SQLite with migrations applied, and `CheckPrFn` stubs. All 452 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.

# Yoke — Build Progress

## feat-process-mgr-heartbeat (2026-04-13)

Implemented `src/server/process/heartbeat.ts` — the two-signal heartbeat for a running child process. The `Heartbeat` class owns a single `setInterval` (tick = `liveness_interval_s` from `ResolvedConfig`). On each tick, it calls `isRateLimited()` fresh (AC-5/RC-2 — suppression state is never cached); if not suppressed, it runs two independent probes: (1) the liveness probe calls `process.kill(pid, 0)` — signal 0 delivers no signal but causes ESRCH if the PID is gone — and emits one `stream.system_notice{subtype:'liveness_stale'}` warning on the first ESRCH (subsequent ESRCH is muted since the session is ending); (2) the stream-activity watchdog computes `Date.now() - lastStdoutAt` and emits one `stream.system_notice{subtype:'stream_idle'}` if idle time exceeds `activity_timeout_s`; the watchdog is reset by `notifyStdoutLine()` so each new idle period can warn once. Neither probe sends SIGTERM or SIGKILL (RC-1). `stop()` calls `clearInterval` (RC-3). Added `liveness_interval_s?: number` (default 30 s) to `Heartbeat` in `src/shared/types/config.ts` and to the schema `$def` in `docs/design/schemas/yoke-config.schema.json` — required to satisfy AC-4 (interval from `ResolvedConfig`, not hard-coded). 22 tests in `tests/process/heartbeat.test.ts` using vitest fake timers and `vi.spyOn(process, 'kill')` cover all 5 acceptance criteria and 3 review criteria; all 392 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.

## feat-process-mgr-ndjson (2026-04-13)

Implemented `src/server/process/stream-json.ts` — the NDJSON line-buffered stream-json parser that
consumes Claude Code `--output-format stream-json` stdout and emits six typed events: `stream.text`,
`stream.thinking`, `stream.tool_use`, `stream.tool_result`, `stream.usage`, `stream.system_notice`,
plus `rate_limit_detected` and `parse_error`. The parser is a `StreamJsonParser extends EventEmitter`
with two input methods: `feed(line: string)` for complete lines (used by the JigProcessManager
readline path) and `feedChunk(data: string)` for arbitrary byte chunks with a `flush()` to drain the
partial-line buffer. Splitting is on LF (`\n`) only — matches the empirical finding that Claude Code
never emits CRLF. Two streaming modes are handled transparently: in default mode, complete `assistant`
events yield `stream.text`/`stream.thinking` with `final:true`; in `--include-partial-messages` mode,
`stream_event content_block_delta` events yield deltas with `final:false` and `stream_event
content_block_stop` emits an empty-delta `final:true` event, while the duplicate `assistant` text/
thinking events are suppressed. `stream.tool_use` is always emitted from the complete `assistant`
event. Rate-limit detection fires when `rate_limit_event.rate_limit_info.status !== "allowed"` with
`resetAt` extracted from `resetsAt`. Malformed JSON lines emit `parse_error` (the Pipeline Engine is
responsible for incrementing `sessions.status_flags.parse_errors` in SQLite on receipt). 39 tests in
`tests/process/stream-json.test.ts` covering all 6 acceptance criteria and all 4 review criteria
against two empirically captured fixture files (`capture-default-mode.jsonl` = research capture-1,
`capture-partial-messages.jsonl` = research capture-4); all 370 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.

## feat-process-mgr-live (2026-04-13)

Implemented `src/server/process/manager.ts` and `src/server/process/jig-manager.ts` — the
`ProcessManager` interface and its production implementation. `manager.ts` exports `ProcessError`
(discriminated kind: `epipe | spawn_failed | stdin_error`), `SpawnOpts` (command + args from
ResolvedConfig only, no hard-coded strings), `SpawnHandle` (typed event overloads: `stdout_line`,
`stderr_data`, `stderr_cap_reached`, `exit`, `error`; plus `isAlive()` and `cancel()`), and
`ProcessManager` (single `spawn(opts)` method — no JigProcessManager-specific leakage). `jig-manager.ts`
exports only `JigProcessManager`; the internal `JigSpawnHandle extends EventEmitter` is not exported.
Key mechanics: `detached:true` gives the child its own process group (`pgid === pid`); `child.unref()`
allows the event loop to exit without waiting (zombie reap documented in a code comment); EPIPE is
caught as a named failure class in the stdin `error` handler (not a generic catch) and re-raised as
`ProcessError{ kind: 'epipe' }`; stderr is capped at 64 KB in the `data` stream handler (slice-before-emit,
never accumulate all bytes first); `stderr_cap_reached` is emitted once; `cancel()` sends SIGTERM to the
entire process group (`process.kill(-pgid, 'SIGTERM')`), waits `gracePeriodMs` (default 10 000 ms),
then escalates to SIGKILL. The SIGTERM→SIGKILL escalation test uses a readiness protocol (child writes
`"ready\n"` after registering its handler) to eliminate the signal-before-handler-registration race. 22
integration tests in `tests/process/jig-manager.test.ts`; all 331 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.

## feat-state-machine-tests (2026-04-12)

Added `tests/state-machine/transitions-assertions.test.ts` — the full 19-assertion
unit test suite for the state machine, mapped 1-to-1 to the §Test assertion list in
`docs/design/state-machine-transitions.md`. All 19 named assertions are present and
none are skipped. Assertion 1 is the completeness test: it defines the 33 documented
non-abandoned pairs explicitly and verifies each returns a defined result from
`transition()`, plus checks that all 27 events are handled by `abandoned` and that
undocumented pairs return `undefined` (no phantom rows). Assertions 2–19 cover valid
to-states, guard semantics (`deps_satisfied` requires `complete` deps, `complete`
requires all four conditions), bootstrap-failed non-auto-transition, phase advance
atomicity, goto/max_revisits, stop-and-ask, user_cancel universality (with the
bootstrapping gap documented), cascade side effects, logging side effects,
idempotency of the crash recovery path (referential equality of pure function
outputs), and stage-level events as workflow-level concerns. 64 new tests; total
309 tests pass; all tests run in ~10ms (pure data-structure assertions, no I/O).

## feat-db-setup (2026-04-12)

Implemented `src/server/storage/db.ts` — the `better-sqlite3` connection pool
that forms the storage foundation for Yoke. `openDbPool(dbPath)` opens a single
writer connection with all four PRAGMAs (`busy_timeout=5000` first, then
`journal_mode=WAL`, `synchronous=NORMAL`, `foreign_keys=ON`), bootstraps the
`schema_migrations` table with `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` (idempotent on
re-open), then opens a separate read-only reader connection with `{
readonly: true }` (enforced at the connection level, not by convention) and
applies `busy_timeout` and `foreign_keys` to it. The module exports only the
`openDbPool` factory and the `DbPool` interface — no module-level singleton.
Added `better-sqlite3` + `@types/better-sqlite3` to package.json. 17
integration tests in `tests/storage/db.test.ts` cover every acceptance
criterion; all 48 tests pass, `tsc --noEmit` clean.

## feat-db-schema (2026-04-12)

Implemented `src/server/storage/migrations/0001_core_tables.sql` — DDL for all
seven core tables (workflows, items, sessions, events, artifact_writes,
pending_attention, prepost_runs) with all indexes and FK semantics exactly as
specified in `docs/design/schemas/sqlite-schema.sql`. The partial index
`idx_pending_attention_open` on `pending_attention(workflow_id) WHERE
acknowledged_at IS NULL` is present and verified by EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN in tests.
All FK ON DELETE CASCADE relationships cascade correctly from workflow deletion;
SET NULL is applied where specified (sessions.item_id, events.item_id,
events.session_id, prepost_runs.session_id, prepost_runs.item_id). The
`prepost_runs.when_phase` CHECK constraint (pre|post) is enforced by SQLite.
`src/server/storage/migrate.ts` provides the `applyMigrations(writer,
migrationsDir)` forward-only runner: skips already-applied versions, wraps each
migration in a transaction, records the version in schema_migrations on success.
42 new integration tests in `tests/storage/schema.test.ts`; all 90 tests pass,
`tsc --noEmit` clean.

## feat-db-migrations (2026-04-12)

Completed the forward-only migrations runner in `src/server/storage/migrate.ts`.
The existing `applyMigrations` skeleton (from feat-db-schema) already handled
discovery, transaction wrapping, and idempotent skip; this session added the two
missing regression guards: (1) a pre-loop RC-3 check that throws if
`schema_migrations` contains a version higher than the highest known file — a
forward-only invariant protecting against deleted or misplaced migration
directories; (2) a per-file AC-3 check that throws when an unapplied file has a
version lower than the highest already-applied version, naming both version
numbers in the error message. The sort comparator was made explicitly numeric
(`parseInt` subtraction) rather than lexicographic to satisfy RC-2. A dedicated
`tests/storage/migrate.test.ts` file (18 integration tests) covers all five
acceptance criteria (AC-1..AC-5) and the three relevant review criteria
(RC-2..RC-4); all 108 tests pass, `tsc --noEmit` clean.

## feat-db-transaction (2026-04-12)

Added `pool.transaction<T>(fn)` to the `DbPool` interface and implemented it in
`openDbPool`. The implementation is a single line: `writer.transaction(fn)(writer)`.
`better-sqlite3` handles BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK internally; if `fn` throws, it rolls
back and re-throws the original exception without wrapping. There is no catch block
in the wrapper — RC-1 is satisfied structurally, not by convention. The wrapper is
injected via `DbPool` (RC-3); no module-level transaction state exists. `fn` receives
`Database.Database` and returns `T`; the outer call returns `T` (AC-5, verified by
`tsc --noEmit`). 15 integration tests in `tests/storage/transaction.test.ts` cover
AC-1 (two-table write atomic after simulated mid-transaction crash, successful
commit, rollback + re-use), AC-2 (same Error instance re-thrown, no message
augmentation, non-Error throws pass through), AC-3 (reader cannot see uncommitted
WAL writes; sees them post-commit; sees nothing after rollback), and AC-5 (typed
return value, fn receives the writer connection). All 123 tests pass;
`tsc --noEmit` clean.

## feat-state-machine (2026-04-12)

Implemented the state machine module in full under `src/server/state-machine/`.
Three new TypeScript modules: `states.ts` (11-element `State` and 27-element
`Event` union literal types), `transitions.ts` (the `TRANSITIONS` const typed as
`{ [S in State]: Partial<Record<Event, TransitionResult>> }` — the mapped-type
key forces a compile-time error when a new State is added without a corresponding
entry; the `abandoned` row is typed as `satisfies Record<Event, TransitionResult>`
so adding a new Event also errors; every row is runtime-frozen via
`Object.freeze`; `transition()` is a pure lookup with no I/O), and
`classifier.ts` (`classify(stderr, parseState): FailureClass` — tags
rate-limit/overload patterns as `transient`, auth/permission patterns as
`permanent`, content-policy patterns as `policy`, and all other patterns as
`unknown`; parse state — parse_errors count + last event type — is a required
input per RC-3 and affects classification when the stream was wholly
unparseable). The `session_fail` TRANSITIONS entry has a conditional result with
three guarded outcomes: `classifier=transient → awaiting_retry`,
`classifier=permanent → awaiting_user`, `classifier=unknown → awaiting_user`,
so `unknown` can never reach a retry path. One known gap filed in handoff.json:
`(bootstrapping, user_cancel)` is absent from state-machine-transitions.md;
the test explicitly documents this as "not in the doc." 122 new tests across
`tests/state-machine/transitions.test.ts` (49) and `tests/state-machine/classifier.test.ts` (73);
all 245 tests pass; `tsc --noEmit` clean.

## feat-config-loader (2026-04-12)

Implemented the synchronous `.yoke.yml` config loader in full. The feature
comprises four new TypeScript modules: `src/shared/types/config.ts` (RawConfig
and ResolvedConfig type definitions, derived from the JSON schema), `src/server/
config/errors.ts` (ConfigLoadError with a discriminated `kind` field for
structured error handling), `src/server/config/resolve.ts` (pure, no-I/O path
resolver that deep-clones the raw config and converts all config-relative paths
to absolute using structuredClone + path.resolve), and `src/server/config/
loader.ts` (the synchronous entry point: readFileSync → yaml.parse → version
pin check → AJV compile/validate → resolveConfig). The project skeleton
(package.json, tsconfig.json, vitest.config.ts, updated .gitignore) was also
created from scratch as no Node project existed. All 31 tests pass; tsc
--noEmit clean.

Until pipeline-engine.ts exists, the SQLite persistence side of this criterion cannot be integration-tested.",
        "AC-4 (rate_limit_detected): The detection pattern (status !== 'allowed') is derived from empirically observed format in docs/research/stream-json-semantics.md §3.6. No actual status='rejected' frame was captured during Phase γ research — the test constructs a frame matching the documented format with status changed to 'rejected'. The pattern must be validated against a live rate-limited session when one is captured.",

        "input_json_delta (tool_use input streaming): deliberately not emitted — the complete tool_use is emitted from the assistant event instead. If the Pipeline Engine needs incremental tool_use input (e.g. for a UI that renders partial inputs), this would require a new stream.tool_input_delta event type.",
        "ScriptedProcessManager integration: the parser is standalone (EventEmitter); wiring it to the JigProcessManager stdout_line events (handle.on('stdout_line', line => parser.feed(line))) is the Pipeline Engine's responsibility when it constructs the session."
      ],

        "Streaming mode detection uses _seenStreamEvent flag: once any stream_event line arrives, ALL subsequent assistant text/thinking events are suppressed. If a future Claude Code version emits stream_event lines for one API call but falls back to default-mode assistant events for another (within the same session), the second API call's text/thinking would be silently dropped. This is considered unlikely given the --include-partial-messages flag is either on or off for the entire session.",
        "content_block_stop emits an empty-delta final event (textDelta=''). Consumers must handle the empty delta gracefully — they should not append it to the accumulated text. The design is consistent with the spec language 'final:true set on the last delta' but 'last delta' here means the stop marker, not the last text-carrying delta.",
        "rate_limit_detected is not emitted for rate_limit_event objects that are missing the rate_limit_info field entirely (the _handleRateLimitEvent returns early). If Claude Code ever emits a malformed rate_limit_event, it would be silently ignored rather than raising a parse_error. This is a gap between AC-1 (malformed JSON → parse_error) and AC-4 (rate_limit detection).",
        "The parser does not reset _seenStreamEvent or _nextBlockId between sessions. If a StreamJsonParser instance is reused across multiple process spawns, the mode flag and block ID counter will carry over from the previous session. Callers must create a new StreamJsonParser for each spawned process."
      ]
    }

        "ScriptedProcessManager (replays captured stream-json JSONL for yoke record fixtures) satisfies the ProcessManager interface but is not implemented here — it is a separate testability feature.",

        "The CheckPrFn default implementation calls 'gh pr list --head <branch> --json number'. This requires the gh CLI to be authenticated and the remote to be GitHub. Non-GitHub remotes (GitLab, Bitbucket, self-hosted) will always get false → cleanup refused if unpushed commits exist. A GitLab/Bitbucket adapter is deferred.",
        "Cleanup does not update the workflows table (worktree_path, branch_name columns) after worktree removal. The Pipeline Engine should null those columns or set status='abandoned' after a successful cleanup. This SQLite write is not in WorktreeManager's scope but must be wired in the Pipeline Engine."
      ],
      "known_risks": [
        "git worktree add requires at least one commit in the repository. If createWorktree() is called on a freshly initialised repo with no commits, git returns an error and createWorktree() throws WorktreeError{kind:'git_error'}. The Pipeline Engine must ensure a repository has at least one commit before invoking createWorktree().",
        "The _hasUnpushedCommits check uses 'git log <branch> --not --remotes --oneline'. In a repo with no remote configured, all commits on the branch are listed as unpushed (--remotes is empty so --not --remotes filters nothing). This is the conservative direction: auto-cleanup is refused unless a PR exists. In projects with no remote (local-only), cleanup will always be refused unless checkPr is overridden to return true or the user uses 'yoke cancel' with --force.",
        "runTeardown() uses fs.existsSync() to check for .yoke/teardown.sh. This is a TOCTOU race (check then act). In production, only the harness and the user's own code write to the worktree, so the race is acceptable. It would become a problem if two concurrent cleanup operations targeted the same worktree, which the Pipeline Engine must prevent by design.",
        "killProcessGroup() sends SIGTERM to -pid (the entire process group). On macOS, if the child has called setsid() or setpgid() to move to a different process group, kill(-pid, SIGTERM) sends to the original pgid (which may be empty if all processes changed groups). This edge case can leave zombie processes. It is the same risk documented for ProcessManager.cancel() and is acceptable in v1.",
        "_runShellCommand uses 'sh -c cmd' with maxBuffer: 4 MB. If a bootstrap command produces stdout exceeding 4 MB, execFileAsync will reject with an ENOBUFS-like error, which _runShellCommand catches and returns as { exitCode: 1, stderr: 'Spawn failed: ...' }. The stderr field will not contain the actual command stderr in this case. This is acceptable for bootstrap commands (4 MB of stdout during npm install is unusual)."

        "Streaming mode detection uses _seenStreamEvent flag: once any stream_event line arrives, ALL subsequent assistant text/thinking events are suppressed. If a future Claude Code version emits stream_event lines for one API call but falls back to default-mode assistant events for another (within the same session), the second API call's text/thinking would be silently dropped. This is considered unlikely given the --include-partial-messages flag is either on or off for the entire session.",
        "content_block_stop emits an empty-delta final event (textDelta=''). Consumers must handle the empty delta gracefully — they should not append it to the accumulated text. The design is consistent with the spec language 'final:true set on the last delta' but 'last delta' here means the stop marker, not the last text-carrying delta.",
        "rate_limit_detected is not emitted for rate_limit_event objects that are missing the rate_limit_info field entirely (the _handleRateLimitEvent returns early). If Claude Code ever emits a malformed rate_limit_event, it would be silently ignored rather than raising a parse_error. This is a gap between AC-1 (malformed JSON → parse_error) and AC-4 (rate_limit detection).",
        "The parser does not reset _seenStreamEvent or _nextBlockId between sessions. If a StreamJsonParser instance is reused across multiple process spawns, the mode flag and block ID counter will carry over from the previous session. Callers must create a new StreamJsonParser for each spawned process."
      ]
    }
  ],
  "prior_features": [
  {
  "feature": "feat-process-mgr-live",
  "entries": [
    {
      "phase": "implement",
      "attempt": 0,
      "ts": "2026-04-13T21:35:00Z",
      "intended_files": [
        "src/server/process/manager.ts",
        "src/server/process/jig-manager.ts",
        "tests/process/jig-manager.test.ts"
      ],
      "deferred_criteria": [
        "stream-json.ts (NDJSON line-buffered parser) and heartbeat.ts (liveness + stream-activity signals) are referenced in architecture.md §4.2 as part of the Process Manager module but are NOT in this feature's scope — they are separate features. The JigSpawnHandle emits raw 'stdout_line' events; the NDJSON parser consumes them from outside.",
        "ScriptedProcessManager (replays captured stream-json JSONL for yoke record fixtures) satisfies the ProcessManager interface but is not implemented here — it is a separate testability feature.",
        "pgid is set to child.pid because detached:true on POSIX makes the child its own process group leader. On Windows, process groups work differently and kill(-pgid, sig) is not supported. Windows support is deferred per plan-draft3.",
        "The cancel() method resolves after sending SIGKILL, not after the 'exit' event fires. Callers that need to confirm the process actually died must listen for 'exit' themselves. This is by design (matches the plan-draft3 shutdown spec) but is a footgun if callers assume the process is dead immediately after await cancel().",
        "No integration with sessions table (pid, pgid, started_at columns) — that wiring belongs in the Pipeline Engine which doesn't yet exist."
      ],
      "known_risks": [
        "EPIPE is caught and emitted as a ProcessError, but the test verifies 'if EPIPE fires, it is typed correctly' rather than 'EPIPE always fires'. Whether EPIPE fires depends on kernel pipe buffer size and timing. On a machine with a large pipe buffer, a 4 MB write might complete before the child destroys stdin, causing no EPIPE. The acceptance criterion is 'does not crash' — verified. The 'named failure class' review criterion is satisfied structurally (the catch clause checks err.code === EPIPE explicitly).",
        "child.unref() unreferences the process handle (the exit watcher) but NOT the stdio stream handles. The readline interface on stdout keeps the event loop alive until stdout is closed (when the child exits). This is intentional — we need to drain stdout — but means the event loop is kept alive by the child's stdio, not the process itself.",
        "gracePeriodMs defaults to 10 000 ms in production. The cancel() timer is a Node.js setTimeout, which may drift under load. Tests use 300 ms with a 50 ms tolerance; production use with 10 s has more headroom. The spec says '10 s grace' — this matches."
      ]
    }
  ],
  "prior_features": [

lines: +        "Pipeline Engine wiring: WorktreeManager.createWorktree() and runBootstrap() are implemented and tested in isolation, but the Pipeline Engine that calls them in sequence and applies the returned BootstrapEvent to transition() does not yet exist. The transition (bootstrapping, bootstrap_ok) → in_progress and (bootstrapping, bootstrap_fail) → bootstrap_failed are in the TRANSITIONS table; the engine must wire them.",

+        "bootstrap_failed → user_retry re-runs bootstrap: transition (bootstrap_failed, user_retry) → bootstrapping with side effect 're-run bootstrap' is specified in TRANSITIONS but the Pipeline Engine that calls WorktreeManager.runBootstrap() again on that transition is not yet implemented.",

+        "Ordered cleanup step 1 (kill tracked pids) is implemented via killProcessGroup(), but the trackedPids list is supplied by the caller (Pipeline Engine). The Pipeline Engine must maintain a list of active SpawnHandle.pid values per workflow and pass them to cleanup(). No integration between ProcessManager SpawnHandle PIDs and the cleanup call is wired yet.",

+        "sessions.pid and sessions.pgid are not written by WorktreeManager — that is ProcessManager's responsibility when it spawns a session. The cleanup step 1 reads trackedPids from the caller; the Pipeline Engine must populate them from the sessions table on startup (crash recovery) or from in-memory SpawnHandle references.",

+        "branch deletion after worktree removal is deferred per plan-draft3 §Worktree Management (D40 §Rules). v1 retains branches. A future feature may add 'yoke cleanup --prune-merged' to delete branches whose PRs are merged.",

+        "The CheckPrFn default implementation calls 'gh pr list --head <branch> --json number'. This requires the gh CLI to be authenticated and the remote to be GitHub. Non-GitHub remotes (GitLab, Bitbucket, self-hosted) will always get false → cleanup refused if unpushed commits exist. A GitLab/Bitbucket adapter is deferred.",

+        "Cleanup does not update the workflows table (worktree_path, branch_name columns) after worktree removal. The Pipeline Engine should null those columns or set status='abandoned' after a successful cleanup. This SQLite write is not in WorktreeManager's scope but must be wired in the Pipeline Engine."

+        "git worktree add requires at least one commit in the repository. If createWorktree() is called on a freshly initialised repo with no commits, git returns an error and createWorktree() throws WorktreeError{kind:'git_error'}. The Pipeline Engine must ensure a repository has at least one commit before invoking createWorktree().",

+        "The _hasUnpushedCommits check uses 'git log <branch> --not --remotes --oneline'. In a repo with no remote configured, all commits on the branch are listed as unpushed (--remotes is empty so --not --remotes filters nothing). This is the conservative direction: auto-cleanup is refused unless a PR exists. In projects with no remote (local-only), cleanup will always be refused unless checkPr is overridden to return true or the user uses 'yoke cancel' with --force.",

+        "runTeardown() uses fs.existsSync() to check for .yoke/teardown.sh. This is a TOCTOU race (check then act). In production, only the harness and the user's own code write to the worktree, so the race is acceptable. It would become a problem if two concurrent cleanup operations targeted the same worktree, which the Pipeline Engine must prevent by design.",

+        "killProcessGroup() sends SIGTERM to -pid (the entire process group). On macOS, if the child has called setsid() or setpgid() to move to a different process group, kill(-pid, SIGTERM) sends to the original pgid (which may be empty if all processes changed groups). This edge case can leave zombie processes. It is the same risk documented for ProcessManager.cancel() and is acceptable in v1.",

+        "_runShellCommand uses 'sh -c cmd' with maxBuffer: 4 MB. If a bootstrap command produces stdout exceeding 4 MB, execFileAsync will reject with an ENOBUFS-like error, which _runShellCommand catches and returns as { exitCode: 1, stderr: 'Spawn failed: ...' }. The stderr field will not contain the actual command stderr in this case. This is acceptable for bootstrap commands (4 MB of stdout during npm install is unusual)."

{
  "feature": "feat-process-mgr-heartbeat",
  "entries": [
    {
      "phase": "implement",
      "attempt": 0,
      "ts": "2026-04-13T22:10:00Z",
      "intended_files": [
        "src/server/process/heartbeat.ts",
        "tests/process/heartbeat.test.ts",

        "docs/design/schemas/yoke-config.schema.json"
      ],
      "deferred_criteria": [
        "Pipeline Engine wiring: the Heartbeat class is complete but is not yet instantiated by anything. The Pipeline Engine must: (1) construct Heartbeat at session spawn time with pid from the SpawnHandle and config from ResolvedConfig phase.heartbeat; (2) pass an isRateLimited callback that reads current item status from SQLite; (3) call notifyStdoutLine() when the SpawnHandle emits 'stdout_line'; (4) forward stream.system_notice events to the Session Log Store and WebSocket; (5) call stop() on the 'exit' event from the SpawnHandle.",
        "per_tool_budgets: the config schema accepts the per_tool_budgets field (from plan-draft3 §Heartbeat D23: per-tool wall-clock budget enforcement) but heartbeat.ts does not yet implement it. The feature spec for feat-process-mgr-heartbeat covers only kill(pid,0) liveness and stream-activity watchdog. Per-tool budget enforcement requires tracking the current tool_use_id from the stream-json parser, which is a separate concern deferred to a future feature.",
        "stream_idle warning fires for stdout silence including silence during long tool_use calls. Plan-draft3 §Heartbeat says 'stdout-silence threshold applies only outside a tool_use'. This nuance (suppressing stream_idle while inside a tool_use) is not implemented. The current implementation warns on any stdout silence regardless of tool_use state. Suppression inside tool_use requires observing stream.tool_use / stream.tool_result boundaries from the stream-json parser — deferred."
      ],
      "known_risks": [
        "Fake timers in tests mock Date.now() via vitest. The Heartbeat uses Date.now() for lastStdoutAt and activityWarnedAt. If vitest's fake timer implementation does not advance Date.now() along with setInterval ticks, the stream-activity watchdog tests would yield wrong idle-time calculations. Verified: vitest fake timers do advance Date.now(); no issue observed during test run.",
        "notifyStdoutLine() sets activityWarnedAt = null unconditionally. If the caller calls notifyStdoutLine() during a rate-limited period (while isRateLimited() returns true), the watchdog warned flag is cleared even though no tick fired. On the next non-rate-limited tick, the idle clock starts fresh (from notifyStdoutLine call time). This is correct behavior but could delay the warning if rate-limiting is lifted after a long silence.",
        "livenessWarnedOnce is never reset. If the Pipeline Engine calls start() + stop() + start() on the same Heartbeat instance (restart scenario), and the PID was previously dead (ESRCH), the liveness probe will not warn again after restart. In practice the Pipeline Engine should create a fresh Heartbeat for each new SpawnHandle (new pid)."
      ]
    }
  ],

The acceptance criterion is 'does not crash' — verified. The 'named failure class' review criterion is satisfied structurally (the catch clause checks err.code === EPIPE explicitly).",
        "child.unref() unreferences the process handle (the exit watcher) but NOT the stdio stream handles. The readline interface on stdout keeps the event loop alive until stdout is closed (when the child exits). This is intentional — we need to drain stdout — but means the event loop is kept alive by the child's stdio, not the process itself.",

content.text:
---

## Summary

**Built:** `src/server/worktree/branch.ts` and `src/server/worktree/manager.ts` — the complete Worktree Manager for feat-worktree-mgr.

**What was built:**

- **`branch.ts`** — four pure functions: `slugify()` (lowercase + hyphen collapse + truncate at 40), `makeShortId()` (strip UUID hyphens, take first 8 chars), `makeBranchName()` (`yoke/<slug>-<shortid>`), `makeWorktreeDirName()` (same, no prefix — filesystem-safe, character set `[a-z0-9-]` prevents path traversal structurally)

- **`manager.ts`** — `WorktreeManager` class:
  - `createWorktree()` — `git worktree add -b <branch> <path>`, writes `branch_name`+`worktree_path` to `workflows` row in a single `db.transaction()` (AC-1, RC-4 path validation)
  - `runBootstrap()` — runs `sh -c cmd` in declared order, stops at first non-zero exit, inserts `pending_attention{kind='bootstrap_failed'}` via transaction, returns `BootstrapEvent` discriminated union for the Pipeline Engine — never calls `transition()` itself (AC-2, AC-3, RC-1)
  - `runTeardown()` — `fs.existsSync` check then `execFileAsync('sh', [script])`, absence is not an error, non-zero exit is non-fatal warning (AC-4)
  - `cleanup()` — pre-checks `git log <branch> --not --remotes --oneline` (remote state, not local refs — RC-2), calls injectable `CheckPrFn`, refuses with warning if unpushed commits + no PR (AC-5), then: kill tracked pids (SIGTERM→poll→SIGKILL) → teardown → `git worktree remove --force` after teardown only (RC-3) → retain branch by default (AC-6)

**Tests:** 26 unit tests (`branch.test.ts`) + 34 integration tests (`manager.test.ts`) using real git repos and real SQLite. All 452 tests pass. `tsc --noEmit` clean.

**Untested / deferred:**
- Pipeline Engine wiring (calls `createWorktree` + `runBootstrap`, applies returned `BootstrapEvent` to `transition()`, wires `user_retry` → re-run bootstrap)
- `trackedPids` population from active `SpawnHandle.pid` values — Pipeline Engine's responsibility
- Non-GitHub PR adapters (GitLab, Bitbucket)
- `workflows` table cleanup after worktree removal (null `branch_name`/`worktree_path`, set `status='abandoned'`) — Pipeline Engine side effect
- Branch deletion after merge — deferred per plan-draft3 (v1: keep branch)

---

## Summary

**Built:** `src/server/worktree/branch.ts` and `src/server/worktree/manager.ts` — the complete Worktree Manager for feat-worktree-mgr.

**What was built:**

- **`branch.ts`** — four pure functions: `slugify()` (lowercase + hyphen collapse + truncate at 40), `makeShortId()` (strip UUID hyphens, take first 8 chars), `makeBranchName()` (`yoke/<slug>-<shortid>`), `makeWorktreeDirName()` (same, no prefix — filesystem-safe, character set `[a-z0-9-]` prevents path traversal structurally)

- **`manager.ts`** — `WorktreeManager` class:
  - `createWorktree()` — `git worktree add -b <branch> <path>`, writes `branch_name`+`worktree_path` to `workflows` row in a single `db.transaction()` (AC-1, RC-4 path validation)
  - `runBootstrap()` — runs `sh -c cmd` in declared order, stops at first non-zero exit, inserts `pending_attention{kind='bootstrap_failed'}` via transaction, returns `BootstrapEvent` discriminated union for the Pipeline Engine — never calls `transition()` itself (AC-2, AC-3, RC-1)
  - `runTeardown()` — `fs.existsSync` check then `execFileAsync('sh', [script])`, absence is not an error, non-zero exit is non-fatal warning (AC-4)
  - `cleanup()` — pre-checks `git log <branch> --not --remotes --oneline` (remote state, not local refs — RC-2), calls injectable `CheckPrFn`, refuses with warning if unpushed commits + no PR (AC-5), then: kill tracked pids (SIGTERM→poll→SIGKILL) → teardown → `git worktree remove --force` after teardown only (RC-3) → retain branch by default (AC-6)

**Tests:** 26 unit tests (`branch.test.ts`) + 34 integration tests (`manager.test.ts`) using real git repos and real SQLite. All 452 tests pass. `tsc --noEmit` clean.

**Untested / deferred:**
- Pipeline Engine wiring (calls `createWorktree` + `runBootstrap`, applies returned `BootstrapEvent` to `transition()`, wires `user_retry` → re-run bootstrap)
- `trackedPids` population from active `SpawnHandle.pid` values — Pipeline Engine's responsibility
- Non-GitHub PR adapters (GitLab, Bitbucket)
- `workflows` table cleanup after worktree removal (null `branch_name`/`worktree_path`, set `status='abandoned'`) — Pipeline Engine side effect
- Branch deletion after merge — deferred per plan-draft3 (v1: keep branch)
